Chapter 1: Banach Spaces

CORE-005

1 Chapter 1: Banach Spaces

The theory of Banach spaces provides the foundational framework for analyzing problems in infinite-dimensional settings. Unlike finite-dimensional vector spaces, where all norms are equivalent and compactness is well-understood, infinite-dimensional spaces exhibit rich and subtle behavior that is essential for modeling continuous systems. This chapter develops the fundamental theory of normed and Banach spaces, establishes key examples that appear throughout mathematical modeling, and demonstrates why completeness is crucial for the existence and uniqueness of solutions to differential and integral equations.

1.1 Normed Vector Spaces

The concept of a norm extends the familiar notion of distance and magnitude to arbitrary vector spaces, providing the analytical tools necessary for studying convergence and continuity in infinite dimensions.

Definition 1.1 (Normed Vector Space). Let V be a vector space over the field F (where F = ℝ or F = ℂ). A function ‖·‖: V → [0,∞) is called a norm if it satisfies: 1. Positive definiteness: ‖x‖ = 0 if and only if x = 0 2. Homogeneity: ‖αx‖ = |α|‖x‖ for all α ∈ F and x ∈ V
3. Triangle inequality: ‖x + y‖ ≤ ‖x‖ + ‖y‖ for all x, y ∈ V

The pair (V, ‖·‖) is called a normed vector space.

Every normed vector space naturally becomes a metric space with the induced metric d(x,y) = ‖x - y‖. This metric structure allows us to define convergence, continuity, and other topological concepts essential for analysis.

Definition 1.2 (Convergence in Normed Spaces). A sequence (xₙ) in a normed space V converges to x ∈ V if ‖xₙ - x‖ → 0 as n → ∞. We write xₙ → x or lim_{n→∞} xₙ = x.

The power of normed spaces becomes apparent when we consider completeness—the property that every Cauchy sequence converges to a point in the space.

Definition 1.3 (Banach Space). A complete normed vector space is called a Banach space. That is, (V, ‖·‖) is a Banach space if every Cauchy sequence in V converges to some element of V.

1.2 Fundamental Examples

The most important Banach spaces in mathematical modeling arise from function spaces and sequence spaces. Each encodes different notions of “size” or “energy” relevant to specific applications.

1.2.1 The Space C[a,b]

The space of continuous functions on a closed interval provides our first concrete example of an infinite-dimensional Banach space.

Definition 1.4 (Supremum Norm). Let C[a,b] denote the vector space of continuous real-valued functions on the closed interval [a,b]. The supremum norm (or uniform norm) is defined by ‖f‖∞ = sup{x∈[a,b]} |f(x)|

Theorem 1.1. (C[a,b], ‖·‖_∞) is a Banach space.

Proof. We must verify that C[a,b] with the supremum norm is complete. Let (fₙ) be a Cauchy sequence in C[a,b]. For any ε > 0, there exists N such that for m,n ≥ N, ‖fₘ - fₙ‖∞ = sup{x∈[a,b]} |fₘ(x) - fₙ(x)| < ε

This implies that for each fixed x ∈ [a,b], the sequence (fₙ(x)) is Cauchy in ℝ, hence convergent. Define f(x) = lim_{n→∞} fₙ(x).

To show f ∈ C[a,b], fix x₀ ∈ [a,b] and ε > 0. Choose N such that ‖fₙ - fₘ‖_∞ < ε/3 for all m,n ≥ N. Since fₙ is continuous at x₀, there exists δ > 0 such that |fₙ(x) - fₙ(x₀)| < ε/3 whenever |x - x₀| < δ. For such x,

|f(x) - f(x₀)| ≤ |f(x) - fₙ(x)| + |fₙ(x) - fₙ(x₀)| + |fₙ(x₀) - f(x₀)| < ε/3 + ε/3 + ε/3 = ε

Thus f is continuous. Finally, ‖fₙ - f‖_∞ → 0 by uniform convergence. □

This completeness is crucial for applications: it guarantees that uniform limits of continuous functions remain continuous, enabling powerful approximation techniques in numerical analysis.

1.2.2 Lp Spaces

The Lp spaces generalize the notion of square-integrable functions and provide natural settings for studying differential equations and optimization problems.

Definition 1.5 (Lp Spaces). Let (X, μ) be a measure space and 1 ≤ p < ∞. The space Lp(X, μ) consists of all measurable functions f: X → ℂ such that ∫_X |f(x)|^p dμ(x) < ∞

The Lp norm is defined by ‖f‖_p = (∫_X |f(x)|^p dμ(x))^{1/p}.

For p = ∞, L∞(X, μ) consists of essentially bounded measurable functions with ‖f‖∞ = ess sup{x∈X} |f(x)|.

Note that Lp spaces are technically defined as equivalence classes of functions that agree almost everywhere, ensuring that ‖f‖_p = 0 implies f = 0 in the norm sense.

Theorem 1.2 (Riesz-Fischer). For 1 ≤ p ≤ ∞, Lp(X, μ) is a Banach space.

The proof relies on Hölder’s inequality for the triangle inequality and Fatou’s lemma for completeness. We omit the technical details but note that completeness of Lp spaces is fundamental to the theory of weak solutions for PDEs.

1.2.3 Sequence Spaces ℓp

The discrete analogues of Lp spaces provide concrete infinite-dimensional examples that illuminate the structure of general Banach spaces.

Definition 1.6 (Sequence Spaces ℓp). For 1 ≤ p < ∞, the space ℓp consists of all complex sequences x = (xₙ) such that ∑_{n=1}^∞ |xₙ|^p < ∞

The ℓp norm is ‖x‖p = (∑{n=1}^∞ |xₙ|p){1/p}.

The space ℓ∞ consists of all bounded sequences with ‖x‖_∞ = sup_n |xₙ|.

Example 1.1 (Computing in ℓ² Space). Consider the sequence x = (1, 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, …). We have ‖x‖2² = ∑{n=1}^∞ = so ‖x‖_2 = π/√6 ≈ 1.28. This sequence belongs to ℓ² but not to ℓ¹ since the harmonic series diverges.

The following Julia code demonstrates computations in finite-dimensional approximations of ℓp spaces:

using LinearAlgebra

function lp_norm(x, p)
    if p == Inf
        return maximum(abs.(x))
    else
        return (sum(abs.(x).^p))^(1/p)
    end
end

# Example: convergence rates for different norms
function demonstrate_lp_norms()
    # Create a sequence approximating (1, 1/2, 1/3, ...)
    n_terms = [10, 100, 1000, 10000]
    
    for N in n_terms
        x = [1/k for k in 1:N]
        
        norm_1 = lp_norm(x, 1)
        norm_2 = lp_norm(x, 2)
        norm_inf = lp_norm(x, Inf)
        
        println("N = $N:")
        println("  ‖x‖₁ = $(round(norm_1, digits=4))")
        println("  ‖x‖₂ = $(round(norm_2, digits=4))")
        println("  ‖x‖∞ = $(round(norm_inf, digits=4))")
        println()
    end
    
    # Theoretical limit for ℓ² norm
    theoretical_l2 = sqrt(π^2/6)
    println("Theoretical ‖x‖₂ limit: $(round(theoretical_l2, digits=4))")
end

demonstrate_lp_norms()

This computation illustrates how different norms capture different aspects of sequence behavior. The ℓ¹ norm grows without bound (the sequence is not in ℓ¹), while the ℓ² norm converges to π/√6.

1.3 Completion of Normed Spaces

Not every normed space is complete, but every normed space can be completed to form a Banach space. This construction is fundamental for extending finite-dimensional techniques to infinite dimensions.

Definition 1.7 (Cauchy Sequence). A sequence (xₙ) in a normed space V is Cauchy if for every ε > 0, there exists N such that ‖xₘ - xₙ‖ < ε whenever m,n ≥ N.

Theorem 1.3 (Completion Theorem). Every normed vector space V can be embedded isometrically into a Banach space V̂ such that V is dense in V̂. The space V̂ is unique up to isometric isomorphism and is called the completion of V.

Proof Sketch. The completion V̂ consists of equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences from V, where two Cauchy sequences are equivalent if their difference converges to zero. The norm on V̂ is defined by ‖[(xₙ)]‖ = lim_{n→∞} ‖xₙ‖, where [(xₙ)] denotes the equivalence class containing the Cauchy sequence (xₙ). □

Example 1.2 (Completion of C₀(ℝ)). Let C₀(ℝ) denote the space of continuous functions on ℝ that vanish at infinity, equipped with the supremum norm. This space is not complete. Its completion is the space of all continuous functions on ℝ that vanish at infinity, denoted C₀(ℝ) or C_b(ℝ) depending on convention.

The completion construction explains why Lp spaces are defined using equivalence classes of functions: the space of Riemann integrable functions with finite Lp norm is not complete, but its completion yields the Banach space Lp(X, μ).

1.4 Schauder Bases

In finite-dimensional spaces, every element can be expressed as a linear combination of basis vectors. The infinite-dimensional analogue requires convergent series rather than finite sums.

Definition 1.8 (Schauder Basis). A sequence (eₙ) in a Banach space V is called a Schauder basis if for every x ∈ V, there exists a unique sequence of scalars (αₙ) such that x = ∑_{n=1}^∞ αₙeₙ where the series converges in the norm of V.

Example 1.3 (Standard Basis for ℓp). The sequence eₙ = (0, 0, …, 0, 1, 0, …) where 1 appears in the nth position forms a Schauder basis for ℓp when 1 ≤ p < ∞. Every x = (x₁, x₂, …) ∈ ℓp can be written as x = ∑{n=1}^∞ xₙeₙ with ‖x - ∑{k=1}^n xₖeₖ‖_p → 0.

Not every Banach space has a Schauder basis. The space ℓ∞ does not have a Schauder basis, which reflects its “non-separable” character.

Definition 1.9 (Separability). A normed space V is separable if it contains a countable dense subset.

Theorem 1.4. If a Banach space has a Schauder basis, then it is separable.

Proof. If (eₙ) is a Schauder basis for V, then the set of all finite linear combinations of the eₙ with rational (or Gaussian rational) coefficients is countable and dense in V. □

The converse is false: there exist separable Banach spaces without Schauder bases, though constructing examples requires sophisticated techniques.

1.5 Finite vs. Infinite Dimensions

The distinction between finite and infinite-dimensional normed spaces is profound and has immediate consequences for modeling and computation.

Theorem 1.5 (Riesz’s Theorem). A normed vector space V is finite-dimensional if and only if its closed unit ball B̄ = {x ∈ V : ‖x‖ ≤ 1} is compact.

Proof. If V is finite-dimensional, then it is isomorphic to ℝⁿ or ℂⁿ for some n, where the closed unit ball is compact by the Heine-Borel theorem.

Conversely, suppose the closed unit ball is compact. We show V must be finite-dimensional by contradiction. Assume V is infinite-dimensional. Construct a sequence (xₙ) inductively: let x₁ be any unit vector, and for n ≥ 2, let xₙ be a unit vector such that ‖xₙ - xₖ‖ ≥ 1/2 for all k < n. (Such xₙ exists by infinite-dimensionality and Riesz’s lemma.) The sequence (xₙ) lies in the unit ball but has no convergent subsequence, contradicting compactness. □

This theorem has immediate computational implications: in finite dimensions, minimization problems over closed bounded sets always have solutions, but in infinite dimensions, additional compactness assumptions are required.

Example 1.4 (Non-compactness in C[0,1]). Consider the sequence fₙ(x) = xⁿ in C[0,1]. We have ‖fₙ‖∞ = 1 for all n, so (fₙ) lies in the unit ball. However, fₙ(x) → 0 for x ∈ [0,1) and fₙ(1) = 1, so the pointwise limit is not continuous. Indeed, ‖fₙ - fₘ‖∞ = 1 for n ≠ m, so no subsequence can converge in C[0,1].

The following Julia code demonstrates this non-compactness phenomenon:

using Plots

function demonstrate_noncompactness()
    # Define the sequence f_n(x) = x^n on [0,1]
    x = 0:0.01:1
    n_values = [1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50]
    
    p = plot(xlabel="x", ylabel="f_n(x)", title="Non-compact sequence in C[0,1]")
    
    for n in n_values
        y = x .^ n
        plot!(p, x, y, label="n=$n", linewidth=2)
    end
    
    # Show the "limit" function (discontinuous)
    y_limit = [x < 1 ? 0.0 : 1.0 for x in x]
    plot!(p, x, y_limit, label="pointwise limit", 
          linewidth=3, linestyle=:dash, color=:black)
    
    display(p)
    
    # Compute pairwise distances in supremum norm
    println("Pairwise distances ‖fₙ - fₘ‖∞:")
    for i in 1:length(n_values)
        for j in (i+1):length(n_values)
            n, m = n_values[i], n_values[j]
            # The supremum norm distance is 1 for distinct powers
            println("‖f$n - f$m‖∞ = 1")
        end
    end
end

demonstrate_noncompactness()

1.6 Applications to Partial Differential Equations

The theory of Banach spaces provides the natural framework for analyzing partial differential equations. Solutions to PDEs typically live in function spaces that must be complete for existence and uniqueness theorems to hold.

Example 1.5 (Heat Equation in L²). Consider the heat equation ∂u/∂t = Δu on Ω × (0,T) u = 0 on ∂Ω × (0,T)
u(·,0) = u₀ in Ω

where Ω ⊂ ℝⁿ is a bounded domain. For u₀ ∈ L²(Ω), there exists a unique solution u ∈ C([0,T]; L²(Ω)) that satisfies the energy estimate ‖u(·,t)‖{L²(Ω)} ≤ ‖u₀‖{L²(Ω)} for all t ≥ 0.

The completeness of L²(Ω) is essential for proving existence via Galerkin approximation: finite-dimensional approximate solutions converge to a solution in the complete space L²(Ω).

The choice of function space encodes physical assumptions about the problem. L² spaces correspond to finite energy, L^∞ spaces to bounded solutions, and fractional Sobolev spaces (Chapter 6) to solutions with specific regularity properties.

Example 1.6 (Variational Formulation). Many PDEs arise as Euler-Lagrange equations for functionals on Banach spaces. The minimal surface equation -div(∇u/√(1 + |∇u|²)) = 0 seeks to minimize the area functional J(u) = ∫_Ω √(1 + |∇u|²) dx over appropriate Sobolev spaces. The existence of minimizers requires compactness arguments that depend on the completeness of the underlying function space.

The following Julia code demonstrates numerical solution of the heat equation using finite differences, illustrating how discrete approximations converge in appropriate norms:

using LinearAlgebra, Plots

function heat_equation_1d(u0, T, nx, nt, α=1.0)
    """
    Solve 1D heat equation u_t = α u_xx with zero Dirichlet conditions
    u0: initial condition function
    T: final time
    nx: number of spatial grid points
    nt: number of time steps
    α: thermal diffusivity
    """
    
    # Spatial grid
    dx = 1.0 / (nx - 1)
    x = 0:dx:1
    
    # Time grid
    dt = T / nt
    
    # Stability condition for explicit scheme
    r = α * dt / dx^2
    if r > 0.5
        @warn "Stability condition violated: r = $r > 0.5"
    end
    
    # Initial condition
    u = [u0(xi) for xi in x]
    u[1] = u[end] = 0.0  # Boundary conditions
    
    # Storage for solution
    solutions = [copy(u)]
    times = [0.0]
    
    # Time stepping with explicit finite differences
    for n in 1:nt
        u_new = copy(u)
        for i in 2:(nx-1)
            u_new[i] = u[i] + r * (u[i+1] - 2*u[i] + u[i-1])
        end
        u = u_new
        
        if n % (nt ÷ 10) == 0  # Store every 10% of time steps
            push!(solutions, copy(u))
            push!(times, n * dt)
        end
    end
    
    return x, times, solutions
end

# Example: Gaussian initial condition
function gaussian_initial(x)
    return exp(-50 * (x - 0.5)^2)
end

# Solve and visualize
x, times, solutions = heat_equation_1d(gaussian_initial, 0.1, 101, 1000)

# Plot evolution
p = plot(xlabel="x", ylabel="u(x,t)", title="Heat equation evolution")
for (i, (t, sol)) in enumerate(zip(times, solutions))
    plot!(p, x, sol, label="t = $(round(t, digits=3))", linewidth=2)
end
display(p)

# Compute L² norms (energy conservation check)
println("L² norms over time:")
for (t, sol) in zip(times, solutions)
    dx = x[2] - x[1]
    l2_norm = sqrt(sum(sol.^2) * dx)
    println("t = $(round(t, digits=3)): ‖u‖₂ = $(round(l2_norm, digits=4))")
end

This numerical experiment demonstrates how the L² norm of solutions to the heat equation decreases over time, reflecting the dissipative nature of the equation. The completeness of L² ensures that numerical approximations converge to the true solution.

1.7 Exercises

Exercise 1.1 (Computational). Implement a function in Julia to compute the distance between two functions in C[0,1] using the supremum norm. Test your implementation with: - f(x) = x² and g(x) = x³ - f(x) = sin(πx) and g(x) = cos(πx) - A sequence of polynomials approximating e^x

Exercise 1.2 (Theoretical). Prove that the space of polynomials on [0,1] with the supremum norm is not complete. Construct an explicit Cauchy sequence that does not converge to a polynomial.

Exercise 1.3 (Computational). For the sequence spaces ℓp with 1 ≤ p ≤ ∞, implement functions to: - Check if a given sequence belongs to ℓp - Compute the ℓp norm - Verify the triangle inequality for specific examples

Exercise 1.4 (Mixed). Consider the space C¹[0,1] of continuously differentiable functions on [0,1] with the norm ‖f‖ = max{‖f‖∞, ‖f’‖∞}. - Prove this is indeed a norm - Show that C¹[0,1] is complete (hence a Banach space) - Implement a Julia function to approximate this norm numerically

Exercise 1.5 (Theoretical). Let V be a normed vector space and W ⊂ V a finite-dimensional subspace. Prove that W is closed in V. Use this to show that in infinite-dimensional normed spaces, there exist closed subspaces that are not complemented.

Exercise 1.6 (Computational). Investigate the non-compactness of the unit ball in ℓ². Construct a sequence of unit vectors such that no subsequence converges, and visualize the first few terms as finite sequences.

Exercise 1.7 (Applications). Consider the integral operator Kf(x) = ∫₀¹ k(x,y)f(y)dy where k(x,y) = min(x,y). - Show that K maps C[0,1] to itself - Compute ‖K‖, the operator norm of K - Implement K numerically and test it on specific functions

Exercise 1.8 (Theoretical). Prove that ℓ¹ is separable but ℓ∞ is not separable. Construct explicit countable dense subsets where they exist.

Exercise 1.9 (Advanced). Let μₙ be probability measures on ℝ converging weakly to μ. Define the space M(ℝ) of finite signed measures with the total variation norm. Investigate whether (μₙ) converges to μ in this norm, and relate your findings to the completeness of M(ℝ).

Exercise 1.10 (Computational Project). Implement a Galerkin method for solving the boundary value problem -u’’ + u = f on (0,1) with u(0) = u(1) = 0. Use the fact that the solution lies in an appropriate Sobolev space (anticipating Chapter 6) to: - Construct finite-dimensional subspaces using polynomial bases - Show numerical convergence in appropriate norms - Compare with exact solutions when available

1.8 References

Conway, J.B. (1990). A Course in Functional Analysis (2nd ed.). Springer-Verlag.

Evans, L.C. (2010). Partial Differential Equations (2nd ed.). American Mathematical Society.

Kreyszig, E. (1978). Introductory Functional Analysis with Applications. John Wiley & Sons.

Reed, M., & Simon, B. (1980). Methods of Modern Mathematical Physics I: Functional Analysis (Revised ed.). Academic Press.

Rudin, W. (1991). Functional Analysis (2nd ed.). McGraw-Hill.

Yosida, K. (1980). Functional Analysis (6th ed.). Springer-Verlag.

2 Chapter 2: Hilbert Spaces

The geometry of finite-dimensional vector spaces extends naturally to infinite dimensions through the concept of inner product spaces. While Banach spaces provide the algebraic framework for functional analysis, Hilbert spaces add geometric structure through orthogonality and projection. This geometric intuition proves invaluable in applications ranging from quantum mechanics to machine learning, where the ability to decompose functions into orthogonal components provides both theoretical insight and computational advantages.

2.1 Inner Product Spaces and Hilbert Spaces

Definition 2.1 (Inner Product Space). Let H be a vector space over the field K (where K = ℝ or K = ℂ). An inner product on H is a mapping ⟨·,·⟩: H × H → K satisfying:

  1. Conjugate symmetry: ⟨x, y⟩ = ⟨y, x⟩* for all x, y ∈ H
  2. Linearity in first argument: ⟨αx + βy, z⟩ = α⟨x, z⟩ + β⟨y, z⟩
  3. Positive definiteness: ⟨x, x⟩ ≥ 0 with equality if and only if x = 0

The pair (H, ⟨·,·⟩) is called an inner product space.

In the real case, conjugate symmetry reduces to symmetry: ⟨x, y⟩ = ⟨y, x⟩. The inner product induces a norm via ‖x‖ = √⟨x, x⟩, which in turn induces a metric d(x, y) = ‖x - y‖.

Definition 2.2 (Hilbert Space). A Hilbert space is an inner product space that is complete with respect to the metric induced by its inner product.

The completeness condition distinguishes Hilbert spaces from mere inner product spaces. This ensures that Cauchy sequences converge within the space—a property essential for analysis.

2.1.1 Examples of Hilbert Spaces

Example 2.1 (Euclidean Space). The space ℝⁿ with inner product ⟨x, y⟩ = ∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ xᵢyᵢ is a finite-dimensional Hilbert space.

Example 2.2 (ℓ² Space). The space ℓ² consists of square-summable sequences x = (x₁, x₂, …) with ∑ᵢ₌₁^∞ |xᵢ|² < ∞. The inner product is ⟨x, y⟩ = ∑ᵢ₌₁^∞ xᵢȳᵢ.

Example 2.3 (L² Space). For a measure space (X, μ), the space L²(X, μ) consists of measurable functions f: X → ℂ with ∫ₓ |f(x)|² dμ(x) < ∞, where functions differing on sets of measure zero are identified. The inner product is ⟨f, g⟩ = ∫ₓ f(x)g̅(x) dμ(x).

using LinearAlgebra, Plots

# Demonstrate inner product properties in ℝ³
function demonstrate_inner_product()
    x = [1.0, 2.0, 3.0]
    y = [2.0, -1.0, 1.0]
    z = [0.0, 1.0, -1.0]
    
    # Inner product in ℝ³
    inner_xy = dot(x, y)
    println("⟨x, y⟩ = $inner_xy")
    
    # Symmetry (for real vectors)
    println("⟨x, y⟩ = ⟨y, x⟩: $(dot(x, y) == dot(y, x))")
    
    # Linearity
    α, β = 2.0, -1.5
    linear_left = dot(α*x + β*y, z)
    linear_right = α*dot(x, z) + β*dot(y, z)
    println("Linearity check: $(abs(linear_left - linear_right) < 1e-12)")
    
    # Cauchy-Schwarz inequality
    cs_left = abs(dot(x, y))
    cs_right = norm(x) * norm(y)
    println("Cauchy-Schwarz: |⟨x,y⟩| ≤ ‖x‖‖y‖: $(cs_left <= cs_right + 1e-12)")
    
    return inner_xy, norm(x), norm(y)
end

demonstrate_inner_product()

The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality emerges naturally from the inner product structure:

Theorem 2.1 (Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality). For any x, y in an inner product space H, |⟨x, y⟩| ≤ ‖x‖ ‖y‖ with equality if and only if x and y are linearly dependent.

Proof. For λ ∈ ℂ, consider 0 ≤ ⟨x - λy, x - λy⟩ = ‖x‖² - λ⟨y, x⟩ - λ̄⟨x, y⟩ + |λ|²‖y‖². Choose λ = ⟨x, y⟩/‖y‖² (assuming y ≠ 0) to minimize this expression. Substituting yields ‖x‖² - |⟨x, y⟩|²/‖y‖² ≥ 0, which gives the desired inequality. □

2.2 Orthogonality and Projection

The notion of orthogonality in Hilbert spaces generalizes the familiar concept from Euclidean geometry.

Definition 2.3 (Orthogonality). Two vectors x, y ∈ H are orthogonal, written x ⊥ y, if ⟨x, y⟩ = 0. For a subset S ⊆ H, the orthogonal complement is S^⊥ = {x ∈ H : ⟨x, s⟩ = 0 for all s ∈ S}.

The orthogonal complement S^⊥ is always a closed subspace of H, regardless of properties of S.

Theorem 2.2 (Projection Theorem). Let H be a Hilbert space and M be a closed subspace of H. For each x ∈ H, there exists a unique element P_M x ∈ M such that ‖x - P_M x‖ = inf{‖x - m‖ : m ∈ M}

Furthermore, P_M x is characterized by the condition (x - P_M x) ⊥ M.

Proof. Let d = inf{‖x - m‖ : m ∈ M}. Choose a sequence {mₙ} ⊆ M with ‖x - mₙ‖ → d. The parallelogram law gives

‖mₙ - mₘ‖² = 2‖x - mₙ‖² + 2‖x - mₘ‖² - 4‖x - (mₙ + mₘ)/2‖²

Since M is convex, (mₙ + mₘ)/2 ∈ M, so ‖x - (mₙ + mₘ)/2‖ ≥ d. As n, m → ∞, we get ‖mₙ - mₘ‖ → 0, showing {mₙ} is Cauchy. By completeness of M, there exists m₀ ∈ M with mₙ → m₀.

Set P_M x = m₀. For any m ∈ M and λ ∈ ℝ, we have m₀ + λm ∈ M, so ‖x - P_M x‖² ≤ ‖x - P_M x - λm‖² = ‖x - P_M x‖² - 2λ Re⟨x - P_M x, m⟩ + λ²‖m‖²

This implies Re⟨x - P_M x, m⟩ = 0. Replacing m with im shows Im⟨x - P_M x, m⟩ = 0, hence (x - P_M x) ⊥ M.

Uniqueness follows from the characterization: if both y₁, y₂ ∈ M satisfy (x - yᵢ) ⊥ M, then (y₁ - y₂) ⊥ M and y₁ - y₂ ∈ M, implying ‖y₁ - y₂‖² = ⟨y₁ - y₂, y₁ - y₂⟩ = 0. □

The projection theorem establishes that H = M ⊕ M^⊥ for any closed subspace M, where ⊕ denotes orthogonal direct sum.

using LinearAlgebra

# Demonstrate orthogonal projection in ℝ³
function orthogonal_projection_demo()
    # Define a 2D subspace M spanned by two orthonormal vectors
    u1 = [1.0, 0.0, 0.0]
    u2 = [0.0, 1.0, 0.0]
    
    # Create projection matrix P = u1*u1' + u2*u2'
    P = u1 * u1' + u2 * u2'
    
    # Vector to project
    x = [2.0, 3.0, 5.0]
    
    # Projection onto M (xy-plane)
    proj_x = P * x
    
    # Orthogonal component
    perp_x = x - proj_x
    
    println("Original vector: $x")
    println("Projection onto xy-plane: $proj_x")
    println("Orthogonal component: $perp_x")
    
    # Verify orthogonality
    println("Orthogonality check: $(abs(dot(proj_x, perp_x)) < 1e-12)")
    
    # Verify Pythagorean theorem: ‖x‖² = ‖proj_x‖² + ‖perp_x‖²
    norm_x_sq = dot(x, x)
    norm_proj_sq = dot(proj_x, proj_x)
    norm_perp_sq = dot(perp_x, perp_x)
    
    println("Pythagorean theorem: $(abs(norm_x_sq - norm_proj_sq - norm_perp_sq) < 1e-12)")
    
    return P, proj_x, perp_x
end

P, proj_x, perp_x = orthogonal_projection_demo()

2.3 The Riesz Representation Theorem

One of the most profound results in Hilbert space theory connects the geometric structure to the algebraic concept of linear functionals.

Definition 2.4 (Bounded Linear Functional). A bounded linear functional on a Hilbert space H is a linear map f: H → ℂ such that ‖f‖ = sup{|f(x)| : ‖x‖ ≤ 1} < ∞.

Theorem 2.3 (Riesz Representation Theorem). Let H be a Hilbert space and f: H → ℂ be a bounded linear functional. Then there exists a unique element y ∈ H such that f(x) = ⟨x, y⟩ for all x ∈ H. Moreover, ‖f‖ = ‖y‖.

Proof. If f = 0, take y = 0. Otherwise, let M = ker(f) = {x ∈ H : f(x) = 0}. Since f is continuous, M is closed, and by assumption M ≠ H. By the projection theorem, M^⊥ ≠ {0}.

Choose any z ∈ M^⊥ with z ≠ 0. For any x ∈ H, the element x - f(x)z/f(z) belongs to M, so ⟨x - f(x)z/f(z), z⟩ = 0

This gives ⟨x, z⟩ = f(x)⟨z, z⟩/f(z). Setting y = z̄f(z)/‖z‖² yields f(x) = ⟨x, y⟩.

For uniqueness, if ⟨x, y₁⟩ = ⟨x, y₂⟩ for all x, then ⟨x, y₁ - y₂⟩ = 0 for all x, implying y₁ = y₂.

Finally, ‖f‖ = sup{|⟨x, y⟩| : ‖x‖ ≤ 1} = ‖y‖ by Cauchy-Schwarz. □

This theorem reveals that every Hilbert space is reflexive: the dual space H* can be identified with H itself through the inner product.

2.4 Orthonormal Bases and Complete Systems

The concept of basis in infinite-dimensional spaces requires careful treatment. Unlike finite dimensions, not every linearly independent set can be extended to a basis in the usual sense.

Definition 2.5 (Orthonormal System). A collection {eᵢ}ᵢ∈I in a Hilbert space H is orthonormal if ⟨eᵢ, eⱼ⟩ = δᵢⱼ for all i, j ∈ I. An orthonormal system is complete (or maximal) if the only element orthogonal to all eᵢ is the zero vector.

Definition 2.6 (Orthonormal Basis). A complete orthonormal system is called an orthonormal basis.

For any finite orthonormal system {e₁, …, eₙ} and x ∈ H, the Fourier coefficients ⟨x, eᵢ⟩ determine the orthogonal projection of x onto span{e₁, …, eₙ}:

P_n x = ∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ ⟨x, eᵢ⟩eᵢ

Bessel’s inequality provides a fundamental bound:

Theorem 2.4 (Bessel’s Inequality). For any orthonormal system {eᵢ}ᵢ∈I and x ∈ H, ∑ᵢ∈I |⟨x, eᵢ⟩|² ≤ ‖x‖²

Proof. For any finite subset F ⊆ I, consider y = ∑ᵢ∈F ⟨x, eᵢ⟩eᵢ. Then

0 ≤ ‖x - y‖² = ⟨x - y, x - y⟩ = ‖x‖² - ∑ᵢ∈F |⟨x, eᵢ⟩|²

The result follows by taking the supremum over all finite subsets F. □

For complete orthonormal systems, Bessel’s inequality becomes an equality:

Theorem 2.5 (Parseval’s Identity). Let {eᵢ}ᵢ∈I be a complete orthonormal system in H. Then for any x ∈ H: 1. x = ∑ᵢ∈I ⟨x, eᵢ⟩eᵢ (convergence in H) 2. ‖x‖² = ∑ᵢ∈I |⟨x, eᵢ⟩|² (Parseval’s identity)

Proof. Let S = span{eᵢ : i ∈ I} and x ∈ H. By completeness, S^⊥ = {0}, so x ∈ S̄. For any ε > 0, there exists y ∈ S with ‖x - y‖ < ε. Writing y = ∑ᵢ∈F ⟨y, eᵢ⟩eᵢ for some finite F, and using that ⟨x - y, eᵢ⟩ = ⟨x, eᵢ⟩ - ⟨y, eᵢ⟩, we find that ∑ᵢ∈F ⟨x, eᵢ⟩eᵢ approximates x arbitrarily well. This establishes (1), and (2) follows by taking norms. □

Example 2.4 (Fourier Series). The functions {e^(inx)/√(2π) : n ∈ ℤ} form a complete orthonormal system in L²[-π, π]. For f ∈ L²[-π, π], the Fourier series

f(x) = ∑{n=-∞}^∞ cₙe^(inx), where cₙ = (1/2π)∫{-π}^π f(t)e^(-int) dt

converges to f in the L² sense, and ‖f‖² = ∑_{n=-∞}^∞ |cₙ|².

using FFTW, Plots

# Demonstrate Parseval's identity with Fourier series
function fourier_parseval_demo()
    N = 256
    L = 2π
    x = range(0, L, length=N+1)[1:end-1]  # Remove duplicate endpoint
    
    # Create a test function
    f = @. sin(2*x) + 0.5*cos(5*x) + 0.3*sin(10*x)
    
    # Compute Fourier coefficients using FFT
    F = fft(f) / N
    
    # Energy in time domain
    energy_time = sum(abs2, f) * L / N
    
    # Energy in frequency domain (Parseval's theorem)
    energy_freq = sum(abs2, F) * L
    
    println("Energy in time domain: $(energy_time)")
    println("Energy in frequency domain: $(energy_freq)")
    println("Relative error: $(abs(energy_time - energy_freq) / energy_time)")
    
    # Plot function and its spectrum
    p1 = plot(x, f, title="Function f(x)", xlabel="x", ylabel="f(x)", 
              linewidth=2, label="f(x)")
    
    freqs = fftfreq(N) * N / L
    p2 = plot(freqs, abs.(F), title="Fourier Spectrum", 
              xlabel="Frequency", ylabel="|F(ω)|", 
              linewidth=2, seriestype=:stem, label="|F(ω)|")
    
    plot(p1, p2, layout=(2,1), size=(800, 600))
end

fourier_parseval_demo()

2.5 Separable Hilbert Spaces

A Hilbert space is separable if it contains a countable dense subset. This property has profound implications for the structure of orthonormal bases.

Theorem 2.6. A Hilbert space H is separable if and only if every orthonormal system in H is at most countable.

Theorem 2.7. Every separable infinite-dimensional Hilbert space is isometrically isomorphic to ℓ².

Proof. Let H be separable and infinite-dimensional. By Zorn’s lemma, H has a complete orthonormal system {eₙ}ₙ₌₁^∞. Define T: H → ℓ² by Tx = (⟨x, eₙ⟩)ₙ₌₁^∞. By Parseval’s identity, T is an isometric isomorphism. □

This remarkable result shows that, up to isomorphism, there is essentially one separable infinite-dimensional Hilbert space. All others—L²[0,1], L²(ℝ), the Hardy space H², and many more—are isomorphic to ℓ².

using Random, LinearAlgebra

# Demonstrate isomorphism between different Hilbert spaces
function hilbert_isomorphism_demo()
    Random.seed!(42)
    
    # Generate random vectors in ℓ²
    n_terms = 100
    x_coeffs = randn(n_terms) ./ (1:n_terms)  # Ensure square summability
    y_coeffs = randn(n_terms) ./ (1:n_terms)
    
    # Inner product in ℓ²
    inner_l2 = dot(x_coeffs, y_coeffs)
    
    # Map to L²[0,1] via Fourier basis
    # f(t) = ∑ x_k * √2 * sin(kπt)  for k ≥ 1
    function f_func(t)
        return sum(x_coeffs[k] * sqrt(2) * sin(k * π * t) for k in 1:n_terms)
    end
    
    function g_func(t)
        return sum(y_coeffs[k] * sqrt(2) * sin(k * π * t) for k in 1:n_terms)
    end
    
    # Approximate inner product in L²[0,1] using quadrature
    function l2_inner_product(f, g, n_points=1000)
        t_points = range(0, 1, length=n_points)
        dt = 1.0 / (n_points - 1)
        return sum(f(t) * g(t) * dt for t in t_points)
    end
    
    inner_L2 = l2_inner_product(f_func, g_func)
    
    println("Inner product in ℓ²: $(inner_l2)")
    println("Inner product in L²[0,1]: $(inner_L2)")
    println("Relative error: $(abs(inner_l2 - inner_L2) / abs(inner_l2))")
    
    # Plot the functions
    t = range(0, 1, length=200)
    f_vals = [f_func(ti) for ti in t]
    g_vals = [g_func(ti) for ti in t]
    
    plot(t, [f_vals g_vals], labels=["f(t)" "g(t)"], 
         title="Functions in L²[0,1]", xlabel="t", linewidth=2)
end

hilbert_isomorphism_demo()

2.6 Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Spaces

Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Spaces (RKHS) provide a bridge between functional analysis and machine learning, offering a rigorous foundation for kernel methods.

Definition 2.7 (Reproducing Kernel). Let H be a Hilbert space of functions f: X → ℝ defined on a set X. A function K: X × X → ℝ is a reproducing kernel for H if: 1. For each x ∈ X, K(·, x) ∈ H 2. For each f ∈ H and x ∈ X, f(x) = ⟨f, K(·, x)⟩_H (reproducing property)

The reproducing property means that point evaluation is a bounded linear functional, represented via the inner product by K(·, x).

Theorem 2.8 (Moore-Aronszajn Theorem). There is a bijective correspondence between positive definite kernels on X and reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces of functions on X.

A key example is the Gaussian kernel K(x, y) = exp(-‖x - y‖²/(2σ²)), which generates an RKHS dense in the continuous functions on any compact subset of ℝᵈ.

Example 2.5 (Gaussian Process Connection). In machine learning, a Gaussian process with kernel K defines a probability distribution over functions. The RKHS corresponding to K provides the “native” space where these random functions naturally live. The posterior mean of a Gaussian process regression lies in this RKHS.

using Distributions, LinearAlgebra, Plots

# Demonstrate Gaussian RKHS and its connection to Gaussian processes
function gaussian_rkhs_demo()
    # Generate sample points
    n = 50
    X = sort(rand(n) * 4π)
    
    # True function (for demonstration)
    f_true = @. sin(X) + 0.3 * sin(3*X)
    
    # Add noise
    σ_noise = 0.2
    y = f_true + σ_noise * randn(n)
    
    # Gaussian kernel parameters
    σ_f = 1.0  # Signal variance
    ℓ = 0.5    # Length scale
    
    # Define Gaussian kernel
    gaussian_kernel(x1, x2) = σ_f^2 * exp(-(x1 - x2)^2 / (2 * ℓ^2))
    
    # Build kernel matrix
    K = [gaussian_kernel(X[i], X[j]) for i in 1:n, j in 1:n]
    K_noise = K + σ_noise^2 * I
    
    # Test points for prediction
    X_test = range(0, 4π, length=100)
    K_star = [gaussian_kernel(X_test[i], X[j]) for i in 1:length(X_test), j in 1:n]
    K_starstar = [gaussian_kernel(X_test[i], X_test[j]) for i in 1:length(X_test), j in 1:length(X_test)]
    
    # GP posterior mean (lies in RKHS)
    μ_post = K_star * (K_noise \ y)
    
    # GP posterior variance
    Σ_post = K_starstar - K_star * (K_noise \ K_star')
    σ_post = sqrt.(diag(Σ_post))
    
    # Plot results
    p = plot(X_test, sin.(X_test) + 0.3 * sin.(3*X_test), 
             label="True function", linewidth=2, color=:black, linestyle=:dash)
    
    scatter!(p, X, y, label="Noisy observations", color=:red, alpha=0.7)
    
    plot!(p, X_test, μ_post, ribbon=2*σ_post, 
          label="GP posterior mean ± 2σ", linewidth=2, color=:blue, 
          fillalpha=0.3)
    
    xlabel!("x")
    ylabel!("f(x)")
    title!("Gaussian Process Regression in RKHS")
    
    # Demonstrate reproducing property numerically
    # Pick a test point and show f(x₀) = ⟨f, K(·, x₀)⟩
    x0_idx = 25
    x0 = X_test[x0_idx]
    f_x0_direct = μ_post[x0_idx]
    
    # Approximate the RKHS inner product using the finite representation
    # In the finite case: ⟨f, g⟩_RKHS ≈ α' K α where f = ∑ αᵢ K(·, xᵢ)
    α = K_noise \ y
    k_x0 = [gaussian_kernel(x0, X[j]) for j in 1:n]
    f_x0_inner = dot(α, k_x0)
    
    println("Reproducing property check:")
    println("f(x₀) direct: $(f_x0_direct)")
    println("⟨f, K(·,x₀)⟩: $(f_x0_inner)")
    println("Relative error: $(abs(f_x0_direct - f_x0_inner) / abs(f_x0_direct))")
    
    return p
end

gaussian_rkhs_demo()

The RKHS framework provides theoretical guarantees for machine learning algorithms. For instance, the representer theorem shows that the solution to regularized empirical risk minimization in an RKHS has the form f(·) = ∑ᵢ αᵢK(·, xᵢ), justifying the use of finite-dimensional representations for infinite-dimensional optimization problems.

2.7 Connections to Other Areas

Hilbert spaces provide the mathematical foundation for numerous areas of mathematics and its applications. The spectral theorem for self-adjoint operators (Chapter 5) generalizes eigenvalue decomposition to infinite dimensions. Sobolev spaces (Chapter 6) combine Hilbert space structure with differential operators to study partial differential equations. In quantum mechanics, the state space is a Hilbert space, with observables represented by self-adjoint operators.

The RKHS theory connects to approximation theory (Chapter 10), providing optimal interpolation and approximation schemes. In machine learning, the kernel trick allows linear algorithms to work in infinite-dimensional feature spaces, while the RKHS provides the proper mathematical framework for understanding regularization and generalization.

2.8 Exercises

Exercise 2.1 (Computational). Implement the Gram-Schmidt process in Julia for vectors in ℓ². Start with the linearly independent vectors v₁ = (1, 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, …), v₂ = (1, 0, 1/3, 0, 1/5, 0, …), and v₃ = (0, 1, 0, 1/3, 0, 1/5, …). Verify orthogonality numerically.

Exercise 2.2 (Theoretical). Prove that in any inner product space, the parallelogram law holds: ‖x + y‖² + ‖x - y‖² = 2(‖x‖² + ‖y‖²). Show that this characterizes inner product norms among all norms.

Exercise 2.3 (Computational). For the Hilbert space L²[0,1], implement numerical computation of Fourier coefficients for the Legendre polynomials. Verify Parseval’s identity for a test function.

Exercise 2.4 (Theoretical). Let H₁, H₂ be Hilbert spaces. Show that H₁ ⊕ H₂ with inner product ⟨(x₁,x₂), (y₁,y₂)⟩ = ⟨x₁,y₁⟩₁ + ⟨x₂,y₂⟩₂ is a Hilbert space.

Exercise 2.5 (Computational). Implement a simple Gaussian process regression solver using the matrix formulation. Compare the computational complexity of direct matrix inversion versus Cholesky decomposition for kernel matrices of increasing size.

Exercise 2.6 (Theoretical). Prove that every finite-dimensional subspace of a Hilbert space is closed. Give an example of an infinite-dimensional subspace that is not closed.

Exercise 2.7 (Computational). Implement the kernel PCA algorithm using an RBF kernel. Apply it to a 2D dataset and visualize the principal components in the feature space.

Exercise 2.8 (Theoretical). Let {eₙ} be an orthonormal basis for a separable Hilbert space H. Prove that for x, y ∈ H, the series ∑ₙ ⟨x, eₙ⟩

3 Chapter 3: Bounded Linear Operators

The theory of bounded linear operators forms the cornerstone of functional analysis and provides the mathematical framework for understanding solution operators in partial differential equations, optimization problems, and machine learning algorithms. This chapter develops the fundamental properties of bounded linear operators between Banach spaces, establishing the crucial principles that govern their behavior and applications.

3.1 Linear Operators and Boundedness

We begin by establishing the basic framework for linear operators between normed spaces.

Definition 3.1 (Linear Operator). Let X and Y be vector spaces over the same field F (either ℝ or ℂ). A mapping T: X → Y is called a linear operator if for all x₁, x₂ ∈ X and all scalars α, β ∈ F:

T(αx₁ + βx₂) = αT(x₁) + βT(x₂)

When working with normed spaces, the concept of boundedness becomes central to understanding operator continuity and well-posedness.

Definition 3.2 (Bounded Linear Operator). Let (X, ‖·‖_X) and (Y, ‖·‖_Y) be normed spaces. A linear operator T: X → Y is called bounded if there exists a constant M ≥ 0 such that

‖T(x)‖_Y ≤ M‖x‖_X

for all x ∈ X.

The fundamental connection between boundedness and continuity provides one of the most important characterizations in functional analysis.

Theorem 3.3 (Boundedness-Continuity Equivalence). Let T: X → Y be a linear operator between normed spaces. The following are equivalent: 1. T is bounded 2. T is continuous 3. T is continuous at 0 4. T is uniformly continuous

Proof. (1) ⟹ (2): Suppose T is bounded with ‖T(x)‖_Y ≤ M‖x‖_X for all x ∈ X. For any x₀ ∈ X and ε > 0, let δ = ε/M (with δ = ε if M = 0). If ‖x - x₀‖_X < δ, then ‖T(x) - T(x₀)‖_Y = ‖T(x - x₀)‖_Y ≤ M‖x - x₀‖_X < Mδ = ε.

  1. ⟹ (3): Immediate, since 0 ∈ X.

  2. ⟹ (1): Suppose T is continuous at 0. Then there exists δ > 0 such that ‖x‖_X < δ implies ‖T(x)‖_Y < 1. For any x ≠ 0, consider y = (δ/2)(x/‖x‖_X). Then ‖y‖_X = δ/2 < δ, so ‖T(y)‖_Y < 1. By linearity: ‖T(x)‖_Y = (2‖x‖_X/δ)‖T(y)‖_Y < 2‖x‖_X/δ. Thus T is bounded with M = 2/δ.

  3. ⟹ (4): If T is bounded, then for any x, y ∈ X: ‖T(x) - T(y)‖_Y = ‖T(x - y)‖_Y ≤ M‖x - y‖_X, which shows uniform continuity with modulus δ = ε/M. □

3.2 The Operator Norm

For bounded linear operators, we can define a natural norm that measures the “size” of the operator.

Definition 3.4 (Operator Norm). Let T: X → Y be a bounded linear operator between normed spaces. The operator norm of T is defined by

‖T‖ = sup{‖T(x)‖_Y : x ∈ X, ‖x‖_X ≤ 1}

This supremum is always finite for bounded operators, and we have the equivalent characterizations:

‖T‖ = sup{‖T(x)‖_Y : x ∈ X, ‖x‖_X = 1} = sup{‖T(x)‖_Y/‖x‖_X : x ∈ X, x ≠ 0}

Theorem 3.5 (Properties of Operator Norm). The operator norm satisfies: 1. ‖T(x)‖_Y ≤ ‖T‖ · ‖x‖_X for all x ∈ X 2. ‖T‖ is the smallest constant M such that ‖T(x)‖_Y ≤ M‖x‖_X for all x ∈ X 3. If S, T: X → Y are bounded linear operators and α ∈ F, then ‖S + T‖ ≤ ‖S‖ + ‖T‖ and ‖αT‖ = |α|‖T‖ 4. If T: X → Y and S: Y → Z are bounded linear operators, then ‖ST‖ ≤ ‖S‖ · ‖T‖

Example 3.1 (Matrix Operators). Consider the linear operator T: ℝⁿ → ℝᵐ defined by T(x) = Ax where A is an m × n matrix. With the standard Euclidean norms, the operator norm depends on the choice of matrix norm. The most common is the spectral norm:

‖T‖ = ‖A‖₂ = √(λ_max(AᵀA))

where λ_max denotes the largest eigenvalue.

using LinearAlgebra

# Example: Computing operator norm for a matrix
function matrix_operator_norm_demo()
    # Define a 3x4 matrix
    A = [1.0 2.0 -1.0 0.5;
         0.0 1.5 2.0 -1.0;
         -1.0 0.0 1.0 1.5]
    
    println("Matrix A:")
    display(A)
    
    # Compute operator norm (spectral norm)
    op_norm = opnorm(A, 2)  # 2-norm (spectral norm)
    println("\nOperator norm ||A||₂ = $(op_norm)")
    
    # Verify the definition: ||Ax|| ≤ ||A|| ||x|| for unit vectors
    println("\nVerifying bound for random unit vectors:")
    for i in 1:5
        x = randn(4)
        x = x / norm(x)  # Normalize to unit vector
        Ax = A * x
        ratio = norm(Ax) / norm(x)
        println("||Ax||/||x|| = $(round(ratio, digits=6)), ||A|| = $(round(op_norm, digits=6))")
        @assert ratio ≤ op_norm + 1e-10  # Allow for numerical error
    end
    
    return op_norm
end

matrix_operator_norm_demo()

3.3 The Space of Bounded Linear Operators

The collection of all bounded linear operators between two normed spaces forms a normed space itself.

Definition 3.6 (Space of Bounded Operators). Let X and Y be normed spaces. We denote by B(X,Y) the set of all bounded linear operators from X to Y. When X = Y, we write B(X) = B(X,X).

Theorem 3.7 (B(X,Y) is a Normed Space). The space B(X,Y) equipped with pointwise addition, scalar multiplication, and the operator norm is a normed space. Moreover, if Y is a Banach space, then B(X,Y) is also a Banach space.

Proof. We verify that B(X,Y) is a vector space and that the operator norm satisfies the norm axioms. The vector space properties follow from linearity. For the norm axioms:

  1. ‖T‖ ≥ 0 is clear, and ‖T‖ = 0 iff T(x) = 0 for all ‖x‖_X ≤ 1, which implies T ≡ 0.
  2. ‖αT‖ = |α|‖T‖ follows from the definition.
  3. The triangle inequality ‖S + T‖ ≤ ‖S‖ + ‖T‖ follows from ‖(S + T)(x)‖_Y ≤ ‖S(x)‖_Y + ‖T(x)‖_Y ≤ (‖S‖ + ‖T‖)‖x‖_X.

For completeness when Y is Banach: Let {Tₙ} be a Cauchy sequence in B(X,Y). For each x ∈ X, {Tₙ(x)} is Cauchy in Y since ‖Tₘ(x) - Tₙ(x)‖_Y ≤ ‖Tₘ - Tₙ‖ · ‖x‖_X. Since Y is complete, T(x) = lim Tₙ(x) exists. The operator T is linear by continuity of the operations, and bounded since for large n, ‖T(x)‖_Y = lim ‖Tₙ(x)‖_Y ≤ ‖Tₙ‖ · ‖x‖_X with ‖Tₙ‖ bounded. □

Example 3.2 (Integral Operators). Consider the Volterra operator V: C([0,1]) → C([0,1]) defined by (V f)(t) = ∫₀ᵗ f(s) ds.

This operator is bounded with ‖V‖ = 1. To see this, note that |(V f)(t)| ≤ ∫₀ᵗ |f(s)| ds ≤ t · max_{s∈[0,1]} |f(s)| ≤ ‖f‖_∞

for all t ∈ [0,1], so ‖V f‖∞ ≤ ‖f‖∞. Equality is achieved by f(t) = 1.

using Plots

# Example: Volterra integral operator
function volterra_operator_demo()
    # Discretize the interval [0,1]
    n = 100
    t = collect(range(0, 1, length=n))
    dt = 1.0 / (n - 1)
    
    # Define a test function f(t) = sin(πt)
    f_vals = sin.(π * t)
    
    # Compute (Vf)(t) = ∫₀ᵗ f(s) ds using trapezoidal rule
    Vf_vals = zeros(n)
    for i in 2:n
        # Trapezoidal integration from 0 to t[i]
        integrand = f_vals[1:i]
        weights = ones(i)
        weights[1] = weights[end] = 0.5
        Vf_vals[i] = dt * sum(weights .* integrand)
    end
    
    # Plot original function and its Volterra transform
    p1 = plot(t, f_vals, label="f(t) = sin(πt)", linewidth=2)
    plot!(p1, t, Vf_vals, label="(Vf)(t)", linewidth=2)
    xlabel!("t")
    title!("Volterra Operator Example")
    
    # Verify operator norm bound
    f_norm = maximum(abs.(f_vals))
    Vf_norm = maximum(abs.(Vf_vals))
    println("||f||_∞ = $(round(f_norm, digits=6))")
    println("||(Vf)||_∞ = $(round(Vf_norm, digits=6))")
    println("Ratio ||(Vf)||_∞ / ||f||_∞ = $(round(Vf_norm/f_norm, digits=6))")
    
    return p1
end

volterra_plot = volterra_operator_demo()
display(volterra_plot)

3.4 The Uniform Boundedness Principle

One of the fundamental results in functional analysis is the Uniform Boundedness Principle, which provides conditions under which pointwise boundedness implies uniform boundedness.

Theorem 3.8 (Banach-Steinhaus Theorem/Uniform Boundedness Principle). Let X be a Banach space, Y a normed space, and {Tᵢ}ᵢ∈I a collection of bounded linear operators from X to Y. If for each x ∈ X,

sup{‖Tᵢ(x)‖_Y : i ∈ I} < ∞,

then sup{‖Tᵢ‖ : i ∈ I} < ∞.

Proof. For each n ∈ ℕ, define Aₙ = {x ∈ X : ‖Tᵢ(x)‖_Y ≤ n for all i ∈ I}.

Each Aₙ is closed: if xₖ → x and xₖ ∈ Aₙ, then by continuity of each Tᵢ, we have ‖Tᵢ(x)‖_Y = lim ‖Tᵢ(xₖ)‖_Y ≤ n, so x ∈ Aₙ.

By assumption, X = ⋃_{n=1}^∞ Aₙ. Since X is a Banach space (hence a Baire space), at least one Aₙ₀ has non-empty interior. Thus there exist x₀ ∈ X and r > 0 such that B(x₀, r) ⊂ Aₙ₀.

For any x with ‖x‖_X ≤ 1, we have x₀ + rx ∈ B(x₀, r) ⊂ Aₙ₀, so ‖Tᵢ(x₀ + rx)‖_Y ≤ n₀ for all i ∈ I.

Similarly, x₀ - rx ∈ Aₙ₀, so ‖Tᵢ(x₀ - rx)‖_Y ≤ n₀. Therefore: ‖Tᵢ(x)‖_Y = (1/2r)‖Tᵢ(x₀ + rx) - Tᵢ(x₀ - rx)‖_Y ≤ (1/2r)(2n₀) = n₀/r.

Thus ‖Tᵢ‖ ≤ n₀/r for all i ∈ I. □

Example 3.3 (Fourier Series Partial Sums). Consider the partial sum operators Sₙ: C(𝕋) → C(𝕋) defined by (Sₙf)(x) = ∑_{k=-n}^n ĉₖ(f) eⁱᵏˣ

where ĉₖ(f) are the Fourier coefficients of f. The Uniform Boundedness Principle shows that the operator norms ‖Sₙ‖ are unbounded, which explains why Fourier series may diverge at individual points even for continuous functions.

using FFTW

# Example demonstrating unbounded Fourier partial sum operators
function fourier_divergence_demo()
    # Create a "bad" continuous function for Fourier series
    # This function will exhibit slow convergence/divergence behavior
    n_points = 512
    x = collect(range(0, 2π, length=n_points+1))[1:end-1]  # Periodic grid
    
    # Construct a continuous but non-smooth function
    # f(x) = sum of carefully chosen terms that create divergence
    f_vals = zeros(n_points)
    for k in 1:20
        # Add terms that decay slowly to create divergence issues
        f_vals .+= cos.(k * x) / log(k + 1)
    end
    
    # Compute Fourier coefficients using FFT
    f_hat = fft(f_vals) / n_points
    
    # Compute partial sums and their norms
    max_n = 50
    partial_sum_norms = Float64[]
    
    for n in 1:max_n
        # Construct n-th partial sum
        S_n_vals = zeros(Complex, n_points)
        
        # Add positive and negative frequency components
        for k in -n:n
            idx = mod(k, n_points) + 1  # Convert to 1-based indexing
            if idx <= n_points
                S_n_vals .+= f_hat[idx] * exp.(1im * k * x)
            end
        end
        
        # Compute operator norm (supremum over unit sphere)
        # For this discrete approximation, use maximum absolute value
        S_n_real = real.(S_n_vals)
        operator_norm = maximum(abs.(S_n_real)) / maximum(abs.(f_vals))
        push!(partial_sum_norms, operator_norm)
    end
    
    # Plot the growth of operator norms
    p = plot(1:max_n, partial_sum_norms, 
             xlabel="n", ylabel="||Sₙ||", 
             title="Growth of Fourier Partial Sum Operator Norms",
             linewidth=2, marker=:circle)
    
    println("Operator norms are growing: $(partial_sum_norms[end] > partial_sum_norms[1])")
    println("Final operator norm: $(round(partial_sum_norms[end], digits=3))")
    
    return p
end

fourier_plot = fourier_divergence_demo()
display(fourier_plot)

3.5 The Open Mapping Theorem

The Open Mapping Theorem is one of the three fundamental principles of functional analysis, along with the Uniform Boundedness Principle and the Hahn-Banach Theorem.

Theorem 3.9 (Open Mapping Theorem). Let T: X → Y be a bounded linear operator between Banach spaces. If T is surjective, then T is an open mapping (i.e., T maps open sets to open sets).

Proof. It suffices to show that T(Bₓ) contains a neighborhood of 0 in Y, where Bₓ = {x ∈ X : ‖x‖ < 1}.

Since T is surjective, Y = ⋃{n=1}^∞ T(nBₓ) = ⋃{n=1}^∞ nT(Bₓ). Since Y is a Banach space, by the Baire Category Theorem, some nT(Bₓ) has non-empty interior. Thus T(Bₓ) has non-empty interior, so there exist y₀ ∈ T(Bₓ) and ε > 0 such that y₀ + εBᵧ ⊂ T(Bₓ), where Bᵧ = {y ∈ Y : ‖y‖ < 1}.

Since -y₀ ∈ T(Bₓ) (as T(Bₓ) = -T(Bₓ) by linearity), we have εBᵧ = (y₀ + εBᵧ) + (-y₀) ⊂ T(Bₓ) + T(Bₓ) ⊂ T(2Bₓ).

By scaling, (ε/2)Bᵧ ⊂ T(Bₓ).

Now we show that (ε/2)Bᵧ ⊂ T(Bₓ). Let y ∈ Y with ‖y‖ < ε/2. We construct a sequence {xₙ} with ∑ ‖xₙ‖ < 1 such that y = ∑ T(xₙ).

Since y ∈ (ε/2)Bᵧ ⊂ T(Bₓ), there exists x₁ ∈ Bₓ with ‖y - T(x₁)‖ < ε/4. Similarly, since ε/4 < ε/2, there exists x₂ with ‖x₂‖ < 1/2 and ‖y - T(x₁) - T(x₂)‖ < ε/8. Continuing this process, we obtain ∑ ‖xₙ‖ < 1 + 1/2 + 1/4 + … = 2, and the partial sums T(∑_{k=1}^n xₖ) converge to y. Since X is complete, x = ∑ xₙ exists with ‖x‖ ≤ 2, and T(x) = y by continuity of T.

Therefore (ε/4)Bᵧ ⊂ T(Bₓ), completing the proof. □

3.6 The Closed Graph Theorem

Theorem 3.10 (Closed Graph Theorem). Let T: X → Y be a linear operator between Banach spaces. Then T is bounded if and only if the graph of T,

G(T) = {(x, T(x)) : x ∈ X} ⊂ X × Y,

is closed in the product space X × Y.

Proof. (⟹): If T is bounded, then T is continuous, so G(T) is closed.

(⟸): Suppose G(T) is closed. Then G(T) is a closed subspace of the Banach space X × Y, hence is itself a Banach space with the product norm ‖(x,y)‖ = ‖x‖_X + ‖y‖_Y.

Define the projection π₁: G(T) → X by π₁((x, T(x))) = x. This is clearly a bounded linear bijection. By the Open Mapping Theorem, π₁ is open, so π₁⁻¹ is continuous. Since π₁⁻¹(x) = (x, T(x)), we have ‖(x, T(x))‖ ≤ C‖x‖_X

for some constant C, which gives ‖T(x)‖_Y ≤ C‖x‖_X. □

Example 3.4 (Closed Graph Application). Consider the differentiation operator D: C¹([0,1]) → C([0,1]) defined by Df = f’. To show D is bounded, we verify that its graph is closed.

If (fₙ, gₙ) ∈ G(D) with (fₙ, gₙ) → (f, g) in C¹([0,1]) × C([0,1]), then fₙ → f uniformly and f’ₙ → g uniformly. By uniform convergence of derivatives, f’ = g, so (f, g) ∈ G(D).

3.7 Applications to Well-Posedness

The three fundamental theorems have profound implications for the well-posedness of operator equations and inverse problems.

Definition 3.11 (Well-Posed Problem). An operator equation T(x) = y is called well-posed in the sense of Hadamard if: 1. For every y in the range of T, there exists a solution x 2. The solution is unique 3. The solution depends continuously on the data y

Example 3.5 (Heat Equation Solution Operator). Consider the heat equation ∂u/∂t = ∂²u/∂x² on [0,1] × [0,T] with homogeneous boundary conditions. The solution operator S that maps initial data u₀ to the solution u(·,T) at time T is bounded as an operator from L²(0,1) to L²(0,1). This follows from energy estimates and the maximum principle.

using LinearAlgebra, SparseArrays

# Example: Discrete heat equation and solution operator bounds
function heat_equation_operator_demo()
    # Spatial discretization
    N = 100
    dx = 1.0 / N
    x = collect(range(dx, 1-dx, length=N-1))  # Interior points
    
    # Discrete Laplacian with Dirichlet boundary conditions
    # Second derivative approximation: (u_{i-1} - 2u_i + u_{i+1})/dx^2
    A = spdiagm(-1 => ones(N-2), 0 => -2*ones(N-1), 1 => ones(N-2)) / dx^2
    
    # Time stepping parameters
    dt = 0.001  # Small time step for stability
    T = 0.1     # Final time
    n_steps = Int(T / dt)
    
    # Heat equation: du/dt = A*u
    # Solution: u(t) = exp(tA) * u_0
    # For explicit Euler: u_{n+1} = (I + dt*A) * u_n
    
    evolution_matrix = I + dt * A
    
    # Compute the solution operator S = (I + dt*A)^n_steps
    S = Matrix(evolution_matrix)
    for i in 2:n_steps
        S = S * evolution_matrix
    end
    
    # Analyze operator properties
    println("Heat equation solution operator analysis:")
    println("Spatial points: $(N-1)")
    println("Time steps: $n_steps")
    println("Final time: $T")
    
    # Compute operator norm and condition number
    op_norm = opnorm(S, 2)
    cond_num = cond(S, 2)
    
    println("Operator norm ||S||₂ = $(round(op_norm, digits=6))")
    println("Condition number κ(S) = $(round(cond_num, digits=6))")
    
    # The heat equation is smoothing, so ||S|| should be ≤ 1
    println("Is operator contractive? $(op_norm ≤ 1.0 + 1e-10)")
    
    # Test with a specific initial condition
    u0 = sin.(π * x)  # Smooth initial data
    u_final = S * u0
    
    # Compute energy dissipation
    energy_initial = sum(u0.^2) * dx
    energy_final = sum(u_final.^2) * dx
    
    println("Initial energy: $(round(energy_initial, digits=6))")
    println("Final energy: $(round(energy_final, digits=6))")
    println("Energy ratio: $(round(energy_final/energy_initial, digits=6))")
    
    return S, x, u0, u_final
end

S, x_grid, u0, u_final = heat_equation_operator_demo()

# Plot the solution evolution
p2 = plot(x_grid, u0, label="Initial u₀", linewidth=2)
plot!(p2, x_grid, u_final, label="Final u(T)", linewidth=2)
xlabel!("x")
ylabel!("u")
title!("Heat Equation: Initial vs Final State")
display(p2)

3.8 Spectral Properties and Resolvents

For operators on Banach spaces, the spectral theory provides crucial information about invertibility and long-term behavior.

Definition 3.12 (Spectrum and Resolvent). Let T ∈ B(X) where X is a Banach space. The spectrum σ(T) is the set of complex numbers λ for which (λI - T) is not invertible in B(X). The resolvent set ρ(T) = ℂ  σ(T), and for λ ∈ ρ(T), the resolvent is R(λ,T) = (λI - T)⁻¹.

Theorem 3.13 (Spectral Radius Formula). For T ∈ B(X), the spectral radius r(T) = sup{|λ| : λ ∈ σ(T)} satisfies

r(T) = lim_{n→∞} ‖Tⁿ‖^(1/n)

The spectral radius determines the asymptotic behavior of operator powers, crucial for understanding convergence in iterative methods and stability of dynamical systems.

3.9 Inverse Operators and Perturbation Theory

When studying inverse problems, the continuity and stability of the inverse operator becomes paramount.

Theorem 3.14 (Neumann Series for Operator Inversion). Let T ∈ B(X) with ‖T‖ < 1. Then (I - T)⁻¹ exists in B(X) and

(I - T)⁻¹ = ∑_{n=0}^∞ Tⁿ

with ‖(I - T)⁻¹‖ ≤ 1/(1 - ‖T‖).

This result provides both existence and a constructive method for computing inverse operators, fundamental in numerical analysis and the study of perturbations.

Example 3.6 (Fredholm Integral Equation). Consider the Fredholm equation of the second kind: f(x) - λ ∫₀¹ K(x,s)f(s)ds = g(x)

where K is a continuous kernel. When |λ| is sufficiently small, this can be solved using the Neumann series, demonstrating well-posedness.

# Example: Fredholm integral equation via Neumann series
function fredholm_neumann_demo()
    # Discretize the integral equation on [0,1]
    n = 50
    x = collect(range(0, 1, length=n))
    dx = 1.0 / (n - 1)
    
    # Define kernel K(x,s) = exp(-|x-s|)
    K = zeros(n, n)
    for i in 1:n, j in 1:n
        K[i,j] = exp(-abs(x[i] - x[j]))
    end
    
    # Scale by dx for numerical integration (rectangular rule)
    K_discrete = K * dx
    
    # Parameter λ and right-hand side g(x) = x(1-x)
    λ = 0.1  # Small parameter for convergence
    g = x .* (1 .- x)
    
    # Operator T = λK, solve (I - T)f = g
    T = λ * K_discrete
    
    println("Fredholm integral equation via Neumann series:")
    println("Operator norm ||T|| = $(round(opnorm(T, 2), digits=6))")
    println("Parameter λ = $λ")
    
    # Check convergence condition
    T_norm = opnorm(T, 2)
    if T_norm < 1
        println("Neumann series converges (||T|| < 1)")
        
        # Compute solution using Neumann series: (I-T)^{-1} = ∑ T^n
        solution_neumann = zeros(n)
        T_power = Matrix{Float


\newpage

# Chapter 4: The Hahn-Banach Theorem and Duality

The Hahn-Banach theorem stands as one of the fundamental pillars of functional analysis, providing a powerful extension principle for linear functionals. Together with its geometric interpretation and the resulting theory of dual spaces, this theorem illuminates the deep connection between linear and convex geometry. The concepts developed in this chapter—duality, weak topologies, and weak compactness—form the theoretical foundation for optimization in infinite-dimensional spaces, variational methods for PDEs, and the mathematical underpinnings of machine learning theory.

## The Analytic Form of the Hahn-Banach Theorem

The Hahn-Banach theorem addresses a fundamental question: given a linear functional defined on a subspace of a normed space, when can we extend it to the entire space while preserving certain properties? The answer leads to profound consequences for the structure of Banach spaces.

> **Definition 4.1 (Sublinear Functional).** Let X be a real vector space. A function p: X → ℝ is called sublinear if:
> 1. p(x + y) ≤ p(x) + p(y) for all x, y ∈ X (subadditivity)
> 2. p(tx) = tp(x) for all t ≥ 0 and x ∈ X (positive homogeneity)

The key insight is that sublinear functionals provide the natural framework for controlled extensions of linear functionals.

> **Theorem 4.2 (Hahn-Banach Theorem - Analytic Form).** Let X be a real vector space, M a subspace of X, and p: X → ℝ a sublinear functional. If f: M → ℝ is a linear functional satisfying f(x) ≤ p(x) for all x ∈ M, then there exists a linear functional F: X → ℝ such that:
> 1. F|_M = f (F extends f)
> 2. F(x) ≤ p(x) for all x ∈ X

*Proof.* The proof employs Zorn's lemma and proceeds by considering the partially ordered set of all linear extensions of f that are dominated by p. Let Ω be the collection of all pairs (N, g) where M ⊆ N ⊆ X, N is a subspace, and g: N → ℝ is linear with g|_M = f and g(x) ≤ p(x) for all x ∈ N.

Define a partial order on Ω by (N₁, g₁) ≤ (N₂, g₂) if N₁ ⊆ N₂ and g₂|_{N₁} = g₁. Every chain in Ω has an upper bound (take the union of the domains and the natural extension of the functionals), so by Zorn's lemma, Ω has a maximal element (N₀, g₀).

It remains to show that N₀ = X. Suppose not, and let x₀ ∈ X \ N₀. Define N₁ = span(N₀ ∪ {x₀}) = {y + tx₀ : y ∈ N₀, t ∈ ℝ}. We seek to extend g₀ to N₁ by defining g₁(y + tx₀) = g₀(y) + tc for some constant c.

The constraint g₁(z) ≤ p(z) for all z ∈ N₁ requires:
g₀(y) + tc ≤ p(y + tx₀)

For t > 0: c ≤ (p(y + tx₀) - g₀(y))/t = p(y/t + x₀) - g₀(y)/t
For t < 0: c ≥ (p(y + tx₀) - g₀(y))/t = -p(-y/|t| - x₀) + g₀(y)/|t|

By the sublinearity of p and the properties of g₀, one can verify that:
sup_{y∈N₀} (-p(-y - x₀) + g₀(y)) ≤ inf_{y∈N₀} (p(y + x₀) - g₀(y))

Choose c in this interval. Then (N₁, g₁) properly extends (N₀, g₀), contradicting maximality. □

### Extension to Complex Spaces

For complex vector spaces, the theorem requires a slight modification since we cannot directly order complex numbers.

> **Corollary 4.3 (Complex Hahn-Banach).** Let X be a complex normed space, M a subspace, and f: M → ℂ a bounded linear functional with ‖f‖ ≤ C. Then f can be extended to a bounded linear functional F: X → ℂ with ‖F‖ ≤ C.

*Proof.* Consider the real part g = Re(f) on M viewed as a real vector space. Apply the real Hahn-Banach theorem with p(x) = C‖x‖ to extend g to G: X → ℝ. Define F(x) = G(x) - iG(ix). One verifies that F is complex-linear and extends f. □

## Applications of the Hahn-Banach Theorem

The power of the Hahn-Banach theorem becomes apparent through its applications to fundamental questions in functional analysis.

> **Corollary 4.4 (Existence of Bounded Linear Functionals).** Let X be a normed space and x₀ ∈ X with x₀ ≠ 0. Then there exists f ∈ X* such that f(x₀) = ‖x₀‖ and ‖f‖ = 1.

This result ensures that the dual space X* contains "enough" functionals to separate points and achieve the norm.

**Example 4.1 (Riesz Representation in ℓ^p Spaces).** Consider the space ℓ^p for 1 ≤ p < ∞. For any y = (yₙ) ∈ ℓ^q where 1/p + 1/q = 1, define fᵧ: ℓ^p → ℝ by fᵧ(x) = Σₙ xₙyₙ. By Hölder's inequality, fᵧ is bounded with ‖fᵧ‖ ≤ ‖y‖_q.

The Hahn-Banach theorem, combined with explicit constructions, shows that every f ∈ (ℓ^p)* arises in this way, establishing the isometric isomorphism (ℓ^p)* ≅ ℓ^q.

```julia
using LinearAlgebra

# Demonstrate the duality between ℓ^p and ℓ^q spaces
function lp_lq_duality_example()
    p = 2.0
    q = 2.0  # For p = 2, we have q = 2 (self-dual)
    n = 100
    
    # Generate random vectors in ℓ^2
    x = randn(n)
    y = randn(n)
    
    # The functional f_y(x) = ⟨x, y⟩
    functional_value = dot(x, y)
    
    # Verify Hölder's inequality bound
    holder_bound = norm(x, p) * norm(y, q)
    
    println("Functional value: $(functional_value)")
    println("Hölder bound: $(holder_bound)")
    println("Bound satisfied: $(abs(functional_value) <= holder_bound + 1e-10)")
    
    # For ℓ^2, the norm of the functional f_y is exactly ‖y‖_2
    functional_norm = norm(y, 2)
    println("‖f_y‖ = ‖y‖_2 = $(functional_norm)")
    
    return functional_value, holder_bound, functional_norm
end

lp_lq_duality_example()

3.10 Dual Spaces and the Canonical Embedding

The dual space X* = {f: X → ℝ : f is bounded and linear} equipped with the operator norm ‖f‖ = sup_{‖x‖≤1} |f(x)| forms a Banach space, even when X is only a normed space.

Definition 4.5 (Canonical Embedding). For a normed space X, the canonical embedding J: X → X** is defined by J(x)(f) = f(x) for x ∈ X and f ∈ X*.

The map J is always a linear isometry, meaning ‖J(x)‖_{X**} = ‖x‖_X. This follows immediately from Corollary 4.4.

Definition 4.6 (Reflexive Space). A Banach space X is reflexive if the canonical embedding J: X → X** is surjective.

Example 4.2 (Non-reflexive Spaces). The space c₀ of sequences converging to zero is not reflexive. Its dual is (c₀)* ≅ ℓ¹, and (ℓ¹)* ≅ ℓ^∞. However, c₀ is isometrically embedded in ℓ^∞ but not onto, since ℓ^∞ contains bounded sequences that do not converge to zero.

# Illustrate the canonical embedding and dual spaces
function canonical_embedding_example()
    # Work with finite-dimensional approximation
    n = 50
    
    # Vector in "c₀" (approximately)
    x = [1/k for k in 1:n]  # This sequence → 0
    
    # Functional from ℓ¹ (dual of c₀)
    y = [(-1)^k / k^2 for k in 1:n]  # This is in ℓ¹
    
    # The functional f_y evaluated at x
    functional_value = dot(x, y)
    
    # Compute norms
    x_norm = maximum(abs.(x))  # ℓ^∞ norm (c₀ ⊂ ℓ^∞)
    y_norm = sum(abs.(y))      # ℓ¹ norm
    
    println("x (approximating c₀): $(x[1:5])...")
    println("y (in ℓ¹): $(y[1:5])...")
    println("⟨x, y⟩ = $(functional_value)")
    println("‖x‖_∞ = $(x_norm)")
    println("‖y‖_1 = $(y_norm)")
    
    # The canonical embedding: x ↦ evaluation functional
    # J(x)(f) = f(x) for any f ∈ X*
    # We can't implement this directly, but we can verify ‖J(x)‖ = ‖x‖
    println("‖J(x)‖ = ‖x‖ = $(x_norm)")
    
    return x, y, functional_value
end

canonical_embedding_example()

3.11 The Geometric Form of Hahn-Banach

The geometric interpretation of the Hahn-Banach theorem reveals its connection to convex geometry and separation of convex sets.

Definition 4.7 (Supporting Hyperplane). Let C be a convex subset of a normed space X and x₀ ∈ ∂C (the boundary of C). A supporting hyperplane to C at x₀ is a closed hyperplane H = {x : f(x) = α} where f ∈ X*, f ≠ 0, f(x₀) = α, and f(x) ≤ α for all x ∈ C.

Theorem 4.8 (Hahn-Banach Separation Theorem). Let A and B be non-empty convex subsets of a normed space X with A ∩ B = ∅. If A is open, then there exist f ∈ X* and α ∈ ℝ such that: sup_{a∈A} f(a) ≤ α ≤ inf_{b∈B} f(b)

This geometric form has immediate applications to optimization theory and the existence of Lagrange multipliers in infinite-dimensional settings.

Example 4.3 (Separation in Function Spaces). Consider the space C[0,1] with the supremum norm. Let A = {f ∈ C[0,1] : f(x) ≥ 0 for all x ∈ [0,1], ‖f‖_∞ < 1} and B = {g}where g(x) = -1. The sets A and B are disjoint and convex (A is open).

By the separation theorem, there exists a bounded linear functional Λ and constant α such that Λ(f) ≤ α ≤ Λ(g) = -Λ(1) for all f ∈ A. By the Riesz representation theorem for C[0,1]*, we have Λ(h) = ∫₀¹ h(x) dμ(x) for some signed Radon measure μ.

3.12 Weak and Weak* Topologies

The topology induced by the norm is often too strong for many purposes in functional analysis. Weaker topologies, defined by families of linear functionals, provide the right framework for compactness and convergence arguments.

Definition 4.9 (Weak Topology). Let X be a normed space. The weak topology σ(X, X) on X is the coarsest topology making all functionals f ∈ X continuous. A net (xₐ) converges weakly to x, written xₐ ⇀ x, if f(xₐ) → f(x) for all f ∈ X*.

Definition 4.10 (Weak* Topology). The weak* topology σ(X, X) on X is the coarsest topology making all evaluation functionals x̂(f) = f(x) (for x ∈ X) continuous. A net (fₐ) converges weak* to f, written fₐ ⇀* f, if fₐ(x) → f(x) for all x ∈ X.

The weak topology is weaker than the norm topology (fewer open sets), while the weak* topology on X* is generally weaker than the weak topology σ(X*, X**).

Example 4.4 (Weak Convergence in Hilbert Spaces). In a Hilbert space H, weak convergence has a particularly nice characterization: xₙ ⇀ x if and only if: 1. (xₙ) is bounded in norm 2. ⟨xₙ, y⟩ → ⟨x, y⟩ for all y in a dense subset of H

using LinearAlgebra

# Demonstrate weak vs strong convergence in finite-dimensional approximation
function weak_convergence_example()
    n = 100
    
    # Create an orthonormal sequence (in infinite dimensions, this would be weakly convergent to 0)
    # In finite dimensions, we approximate with "nearly orthogonal" vectors
    
    # Standard basis vectors (these are orthonormal)
    e_vectors = [zeros(n) for _ in 1:min(n, 20)]
    for i in 1:min(n, 20)
        e_vectors[i][i] = 1.0
    end
    
    # These vectors don't converge strongly (‖eᵢ - eⱼ‖ = √2 for i ≠ j)
    # But they converge weakly to 0 in infinite dimensions
    
    println("Strong norms of basis vectors:")
    for i in 1:min(5, length(e_vectors))
        println("‖e_$(i)‖ = $(norm(e_vectors[i]))")
    end
    
    println("\nPairwise distances:")
    for i in 1:min(3, length(e_vectors)-1)
        for j in i+1:min(i+2, length(e_vectors))
            distance = norm(e_vectors[i] - e_vectors[j])
            println("‖e_$(i) - e_$(j)‖ = $(distance)")
        end
    end
    
    # Weak convergence: ⟨eᵢ, v⟩ → ⟨0, v⟩ = 0 for fixed v
    v = randn(n)
    v = v / norm(v)  # normalize
    
    println("\nWeak convergence to 0: ⟨eᵢ, v⟩ for random v:")
    for i in 1:min(10, length(e_vectors))
        inner_product = dot(e_vectors[i], v)
        println("⟨e_$(i), v⟩ = $(inner_product)")
    end
    
    return e_vectors, v
end

weak_convergence_example()

3.13 The Banach-Alaoglu Theorem

One of the most important consequences of weak* topology is the compactness of the unit ball in dual spaces.

Theorem 4.11 (Banach-Alaoglu). Let X be a normed space. The closed unit ball B* = {f ∈ X* : ‖f‖ ≤ 1} is compact in the weak* topology σ(X*, X).

Proof. By Tychonoff’s theorem, it suffices to show that B* is a closed subset of a compact product space. For each x ∈ X with ‖x‖ ≤ 1, consider the interval [-1, 1] ⊂ ℝ. The product space ∏_{‖x‖≤1} [-1, 1] is compact by Tychonoff’s theorem.

Define Φ: B* → ∏{‖x‖≤1} [-1, 1] by Φ(f) = (f(x)){‖x‖≤1}. This map is injective (if two functionals agree on the unit ball, they agree everywhere) and continuous in the weak* topology.

The image Φ(B) consists of those functions φ: {x : ‖x‖ ≤ 1} → [-1, 1] that are linear and bounded. This is a closed subset of the product space, hence compact. Since Φ is a homeomorphism onto its image, B is compact in the weak* topology. □

This theorem has profound implications for optimization and the existence of solutions to variational problems.

Example 4.5 (Application to Optimization). Consider the optimization problem: minimize J(f) = ∫₀¹ L(x, f(x), f’(x)) dx subject to f ∈ W^{1,p}(0,1), ‖f‖_{W^{1,p}} ≤ M

If L satisfies appropriate growth and coercivity conditions, the Banach-Alaoglu theorem (applied in the dual space setting through reflexivity) guarantees the existence of minimizers via weak compactness arguments.

# Simulate weak* compactness in finite dimensions
function banach_alaoglu_simulation()
    n = 50  # Dimension of the space
    
    # Generate a sequence of functionals on ℝⁿ (represented as vectors)
    # Each functional f corresponds to f(x) = ⟨f, x⟩
    num_functionals = 1000
    
    # Create bounded functionals (unit ball in dual space)
    functionals = [randn(n) for _ in 1:num_functionals]
    for i in 1:num_functionals
        functionals[i] = functionals[i] / max(norm(functionals[i]), 1.0)  # Project to unit ball
    end
    
    # Test weak* convergence for a subsequence
    # In infinite dimensions, we'd have weak* cluster points
    
    # Pick a few test vectors
    test_vectors = [randn(n) for _ in 1:5]
    for v in test_vectors
        v ./= norm(v)  # normalize
    end
    
    println("Testing weak* behavior:")
    println("First few functionals evaluated on test vector v₁:")
    v1 = test_vectors[1]
    
    for i in 1:min(10, num_functionals)
        value = dot(functionals[i], v1)
        println("f_$(i)(v₁) = $(round(value, digits=4))")
    end
    
    # In a truly infinite-dimensional setting with weak* topology,
    # every bounded sequence would have a weak* convergent subsequence
    
    # Demonstrate that strong convergence would be impossible
    println("\nStrong distances between functionals (showing no strong convergence):")
    for i in 1:min(5, num_functionals-1)
        distance = norm(functionals[i] - functionals[i+1])
        println("‖f_$(i) - f_$(i+1)‖ = $(round(distance, digits=4))")
    end
    
    return functionals, test_vectors
end

banach_alaoglu_simulation()

3.14 Reflexivity and Weak Compactness

The connection between reflexivity and weak compactness provides one of the most elegant characterizations in functional analysis.

Theorem 4.12 (Eberlein-Šmulian). A Banach space X is reflexive if and only if every bounded sequence in X has a weakly convergent subsequence.

Corollary 4.13 (Weak Compactness of the Unit Ball). In a reflexive Banach space, the closed unit ball is weakly compact.

These results are fundamental for the calculus of variations and optimization theory in infinite dimensions.

Example 4.6 (Weak Compactness in Sobolev Spaces). The Sobolev space W^{1,p}(Ω) for 1 < p < ∞ is reflexive. This means that any bounded sequence of functions {uₙ} ⊂ W^{1,p}(Ω) has a subsequence that converges weakly to some u ∈ W^{1,p}(Ω). This weak compactness is crucial for proving existence of solutions to elliptic PDEs via the direct method of the calculus of variations.

3.15 Applications to Convex Optimization

The Hahn-Banach theorem and weak* compactness provide the foundation for duality theory in convex optimization.

Definition 4.14 (Convex Optimization Problem). Let X be a normed space, f: X → ℝ ∪ {+∞} a proper convex function, and C ⊂ X a convex set. The primal problem is: minimize f(x) subject to x ∈ C

The dual problem involves the convex conjugate f(x) = sup_{x∈X} {⟨x*, x⟩ - f(x)}.

Theorem 4.15 (Weak Duality). For the primal and dual problems above, we always have: inf (primal) ≥ sup (dual)

Under appropriate constraint qualifications (often involving the Hahn-Banach theorem to separate sets), strong duality holds: the infimum and supremum are equal and achieved.

Example 4.7 (Lagrange Multipliers in Hilbert Space). Consider minimizing ½‖x‖² subject to Ax = b where A: H₁ → H₂ is a bounded linear operator between Hilbert spaces. If the range of A is closed, the Hahn-Banach theorem guarantees the existence of Lagrange multipliers λ ∈ H₂ such that x + A*λ = 0 and Ax = b at the minimizer.

using LinearAlgebra, SparseArrays

# Demonstrate duality in finite-dimensional quadratic programming
function quadratic_programming_duality()
    n = 20
    m = 10
    
    # Generate problem data: minimize (1/2)x'Qx + c'x subject to Ax = b
    Q = randn(n, n)
    Q = Q'Q + I  # Make positive definite
    c = randn(n)
    A = randn(m, n)
    b = randn(m)
    
    # Solve primal problem: x* = argmin (1/2)x'Qx + c'x subject to Ax = b
    # Using method of Lagrange multipliers: ∇_x L = Qx + c + A'λ = 0
    # Constraint: Ax = b
    
    # Form KKT system: [Q A'] [x] = [-c]
    #                  [A 0 ] [λ]   [b ]
    
    KKT_matrix = [Q A'; A zeros(m, m)]
    rhs = [-c; b]
    solution = KKT_matrix \ rhs
    
    x_star = solution[1:n]
    lambda_star = solution[n+1:end]
    
    # Verify optimality conditions
    primal_objective = 0.5 * dot(x_star, Q * x_star) + dot(c, x_star)
    constraint_violation = norm(A * x_star - b)
    lagrangian_gradient = Q * x_star + c + A' * lambda_star
    
    println("Primal objective: $(round(primal_objective, digits=6))")
    println("Constraint violation: $(round(constraint_violation, digits=10))")
    println("Lagrangian gradient norm: $(round(norm(lagrangian_gradient), digits=10))")
    
    # Dual function: g(λ) = inf_x L(x,λ) = -(1/2)(c + A'λ)'Q⁻¹(c + A'λ) + b'λ
    function dual_function(lambda)
        grad_term = c + A' * lambda
        return -0.5 * dot(grad_term, Q \ grad_term) + dot(b, lambda)
    end
    
    dual_objective = dual_function(lambda_star)
    duality_gap = primal_objective - dual_objective
    
    println("Dual objective: $(round(dual_objective, digits=6))")
    println("Duality gap: $(round(duality_gap, digits=10))")
    
    return x_star, lambda_star, primal_objective, dual_objective
end

quadratic_programming_duality()

3.16 Connections to Variational Problems

The weak topologies and compactness results developed in this chapter are essential for the existence theory of variational problems, particularly in the context of partial differential equations.

Consider the Dirichlet problem for the Laplace equation: -Δu = f in Ω, u = 0 on ∂Ω

The weak formulation seeks u ∈ H₀¹(Ω) such that ∫_Ω ∇u · ∇v dx = ∫_Ω fv dx for all v ∈ H₀¹(Ω). The existence of solutions follows from:

  1. Coercivity: The bilinear form a(u,v) = ∫Ω ∇u · ∇v dx satisfies a(u,u) ≥ α‖u‖²{H₀¹} for some α > 0
  2. Continuity: |a(u,v)| ≤ C‖u‖{H₀¹}‖v‖{H₀¹}
  3. Riesz Representation: In the Hilbert space H₀¹(Ω), every bounded linear functional has the form v ↦ ⟨u, v⟩ for some u

The Hahn-Banach theorem ensures that the linear functional L(v) = ∫_Ω fv dx extends to all of H₀¹(Ω) when f ∈ L²(Ω).

3.17 Exercises

Exercise 4.1 (Proof-based). Let X be a normed space and M a finite-dimensional subspace. Show that every linear functional f: M → ℝ has a norm-preserving extension to X. (Hint: Use the fact that all norms on finite-dimensional spaces are equivalent.)

Exercise 4.2 (Julia Implementation). Implement a function that numerically verifies the Hahn-Banach extension property for functionals on ℓ² spaces. Given a functional defined on a finite-dimensional subspace, construct an extension to the full space.

function verify_hahn_banach_extension(subspace_dim, total_dim, num_tests=100)
    # Your implementation here
    # Test the extension property for random functionals
end

Exercise 4.3 (Theoretical). Prove that c₀ is not reflexive by showing explicitly that the canonical embedding J: c₀ → (c₀)** is not surjective. Construct a functional in (c₀)** that is not of the form J(x) for any x ∈ c₀.

Exercise 4.4 (Julia Implementation). Write a function to simulate weak convergence in finite-dimensional approximations of ℓ² spaces. Demonstrate that the standard basis vectors {eₙ} satisfy the properties of weak convergence to zero.

Exercise 4.5 (Application). Consider the optimization problem: minimize ∫₀¹ |u’(x)|² dx subject to ∫₀¹ u(x) dx = 1 and u(0) = u(1) = 0. Use the Lagrange multiplier method in the Hilbert space H₀¹(0,1) to find the optimal solution. Verify your answer using the calculus of variations.

Exercise 4.6 (Proof-based). Let X be a reflexive Banach space and {xₙ} a bounded sequence in X. Prove that if every weakly convergent subsequence of {xₙ} has the same weak limit x, then xₙ ⇀ x.

Exercise 4.7 (Julia Implementation). Implement the dual simplex method for linear programming, demonstrating strong duality between the primal and dual problems.

Exercise 4.8 (Theoretical). Show that in a non-reflexive space, the closed unit ball is not weakly compact. Specifically, construct a sequence in the unit ball of c₀ that has no weakly convergent subsequence.

Exercise 4.9 (Advanced). Let X be a Banach space and K ⊂ X a weakly compact convex set. Prove that every continuous convex functional on K achieves its minimum. Apply this to show existence of solutions for variational problems of the form: minimize J(u) subject to u ∈ K where J is convex and weakly lower semicontinuous.

Exercise 4.10 (Julia Implementation). Implement a numerical method for solving the dual formulation of the minimum norm problem: find x ∈ H with minimal ‖x‖ subject to Ax = b. Compare the computational efficiency of primal versus dual approaches.

3.18 References

Brezis, H. (2011). Functional Analysis, Sobolev Spaces and Partial Differential Equations. Springer.

Conway, J. B. (2007). A Course in Functional Analysis. Springer.

Dunford, N., & Schwartz, J. T. (1958). Linear Operators, Part I: General Theory. Wiley.

Ekeland, I., & Témam, R. (1999). Convex Analysis and Variational Problems. SIAM.

Evans, L. C. (2010). Partial Differential Equations. American Mathematical Society.

Rockafellar, R. T. (1970). Convex Analysis. Princeton University Press.

Rudin, W. (1991). Functional Analysis. McGraw-Hill.

Yosida, K. (1995). Functional Analysis. Springer.

4 Chapter 5: Compact Operators and Spectral Theory

4.1 5.1 Introduction to Compact Operators

Compact operators represent one of the most elegant and powerful classes of operators in functional analysis, bridging the gap between finite-dimensional and infinite-dimensional linear algebra. These operators exhibit spectral properties remarkably similar to matrices while operating on infinite-dimensional spaces, making them indispensable tools in partial differential equations, integral equations, and modern machine learning applications.

Definition 5.1 (Compact Operator). Let X and Y be normed spaces, and let T: X → Y be a bounded linear operator. We say T is compact if for every bounded sequence (x_n) in X, the sequence (Tx_n) contains a convergent subsequence in Y.

Equivalently, T is compact if it maps bounded sets to relatively compact sets (sets whose closure is compact). This definition immediately reveals why compact operators are so tractable: they transform the infinite-dimensional complexity of bounded sets into the manageable structure of compact sets.

Theorem 5.1 (Characterization of Compact Operators). Let T: X → Y be a bounded linear operator between Banach spaces. The following are equivalent: 1. T is compact. 2. T maps the unit ball of X to a relatively compact set in Y. 3. For every bounded sequence (x_n) that converges weakly to x, we have Tx_n → Tx in norm.
