generates a Markov semigroup on L²(ℝ,γ) where γ is the invariant Gaussian measure. The generator is

Af = -αx f' + (σ²/2)f''

This semigroup has the explicit representation:

(S(t)f)(x) = ∫_{-∞}^∞ f(xe^{-αt} + y) (2πσ²(1-e^{-2αt})/α)^{-1/2} exp(-αy²/(2σ²(1-e^{-2αt}))) dy
using Distributions, Random

function ornstein_uhlenbeck_semigroup(f_values, x_grid, t, alpha, sigma)
    """
    Apply Ornstein-Uhlenbeck semigroup to discretized function.
    
    Parameters:
    - f_values: function values on grid
    - x_grid: spatial grid points  
    - t: time parameter
    - alpha: drift coefficient
    - sigma: diffusion coefficient
    """
    n = length(x_grid)
    result = zeros(n)
    
    # Transition parameters
    mean_coeff = exp(-alpha * t)
    var_coeff = sigma^2 * (1 - exp(-2*alpha*t)) / (2*alpha)
    
    for (i, x) in enumerate(x_grid)
        # For each final position x, integrate over initial positions
        integrand = 0.0
        
        for (j, y) in enumerate(x_grid)
            # Transition density from y to x
            mean_final = y * mean_coeff
            transition_density = pdf(Normal(mean_final, sqrt(var_coeff)), x)
            
            # Add contribution (trapezoidal rule)
            if j == 1 || j == n
                weight = 0.5
            else
                weight = 1.0
            end
            
            dx = x_grid[2] - x_grid[1]  # Assuming


\newpage

# Chapter 9: Distribution Theory

The theory of distributions, also known as generalized functions, provides a rigorous mathematical framework for handling singular objects that arise naturally in differential equations and physics but cannot be treated as classical functions. Consider the Dirac delta "function" δ(x), which models a unit point mass at the origin. While δ(x) = 0 for x ≠ 0 and ∫δ(x)dx = 1, no classical function satisfies these properties. Distribution theory resolves this paradox by treating δ not as a function, but as a continuous linear functional on an appropriate space of test functions.

This chapter develops the theory of distributions from first principles, establishing the spaces of test functions and tempered distributions, studying operations like differentiation and convolution, and culminating in applications to partial differential equations and fundamental solutions. The power of distribution theory lies in its ability to make rigorous the informal manipulations commonly used in applied mathematics and physics.

## Test Functions and Distributions

### The Space of Test Functions

The foundation of distribution theory rests on carefully chosen spaces of smooth functions with compact support.

> **Definition 9.1 (Test Functions).** The space of test functions D(Ω) consists of all functions φ: Ω → ℝ that are infinitely differentiable (C∞) and have compact support in the open set Ω ⊆ ℝⁿ. We denote D(ℝⁿ) simply as D.

The compact support condition means that for each φ ∈ D(Ω), there exists a compact set K ⊆ Ω such that φ(x) = 0 for all x ∉ K. This ensures that test functions vanish outside bounded regions along with all their derivatives.

A sequence {φⱼ} ⊆ D(Ω) converges to φ ∈ D(Ω) in the test function topology if:
1. There exists a compact set K ⊆ Ω such that supp(φⱼ) ⊆ K for all j
2. For each multi-index α, ∂ᵅφⱼ converges uniformly to ∂ᵅφ on K

This topology makes D(Ω) a complete topological vector space, though not metrizable.

### Distributions as Linear Functionals

> **Definition 9.2 (Distribution).** A distribution (or generalized function) on Ω is a continuous linear functional T: D(Ω) → ℝ. The space of all distributions on Ω is denoted D'(Ω).

The action of a distribution T on a test function φ is written ⟨T, φ⟩. For a locally integrable function f, the associated regular distribution is defined by ⟨Tf, φ⟩ = ∫Ω f(x)φ(x)dx.

> **Example 9.1 (Dirac Delta Distribution).** The Dirac delta distribution δ is defined by ⟨δ, φ⟩ = φ(0) for all φ ∈ D. This is linear and continuous, making δ ∈ D'(ℝⁿ). More generally, the point mass at a ∈ ℝⁿ is δₐ: ⟨δₐ, φ⟩ = φ(a).

```julia
using QuadGK, Plots

# Approximate the Dirac delta with a sequence of functions
function delta_approximation(x, ε)
    return (1/(ε * sqrt(π))) * exp(-(x/ε)^2)
end

# Test the approximation property
function test_delta_approximation(ε_values)
    x = -3:0.01:3
    φ(x) = exp(-x^2) * cos(2π*x)  # A test function
    
    results = Float64[]
    
    for ε in ε_values
        # Approximate ∫ δ_ε(x) φ(x) dx
        integrand(t) = delta_approximation(t, ε) * φ(t)
        integral, _ = quadgk(integrand, -10, 10)
        push!(results, integral)
    end
    
    exact_value = φ(0)  # ⟨δ, φ⟩ = φ(0)
    
    println("Approximation of ⟨δ, φ⟩:")
    println("Exact value: ", exact_value)
    for (ε, result) in zip(ε_values, results)
        println("ε = $ε: $result (error: $(abs(result - exact_value)))")
    end
    
    return results
end

ε_values = [1.0, 0.5, 0.2, 0.1, 0.05]
test_delta_approximation(ε_values)

Theorem 9.1 (Support of a Distribution). For T ∈ D’(Ω), the support of T is the smallest closed set outside which T vanishes in the distributional sense. Formally, supp(T) is the complement of the largest open set U such that ⟨T, φ⟩ = 0 for all φ ∈ D with supp(φ) ⊆ U.

Proof. The collection of open sets {U ⊆ Ω : ⟨T, φ⟩ = 0 for all φ ∈ D with supp(φ) ⊆ U} has a maximal element by Zorn’s lemma. The complement of this maximal set is the support. □

6.9 Derivatives of Distributions

One of the most powerful features of distribution theory is that every distribution possesses derivatives of all orders. This extends differentiation beyond the classical setting where derivatives may fail to exist.

Definition 9.3 (Distributional Derivative). For T ∈ D’(Ω) and multi-index α, the αth distributional derivative ∂ᵅT is defined by ⟨∂ᵅT, φ⟩ = (-1)^|α|⟨T, ∂ᵅφ⟩ for all φ ∈ D(Ω).

The sign convention ensures that for smooth functions f, the distributional derivative coincides with the classical derivative when it exists.

Example 9.2 (Derivative of the Heaviside Function). Let H(x) = 1 for x > 0 and H(x) = 0 for x < 0. Then ⟨H’, φ⟩ = -⟨H, φ’⟩ = -∫₀^∞ φ’(x)dx = φ(0) = ⟨δ, φ⟩. Therefore H’ = δ in the distributional sense.

This remarkable result shows that the derivative of the discontinuous Heaviside function is the Dirac delta distribution.

Theorem 9.2 (Properties of Distributional Derivatives). Let T, S ∈ D’(Ω), c ∈ ℝ, and α, β be multi-indices. Then: 1. ∂ᵅ(cT + S) = c∂ᵅT + ∂ᵅS (linearity) 2. ∂ᵅ∂ᵝT = ∂ᵅ⁺ᵝT (mixed derivatives commute) 3. If f ∈ C^∞(Ω), then ∂ᵅ(fT) = Σₖ₌₀^α (α choose γ)(∂ᵞf)(∂ᵅ⁻ᵞT) (Leibniz rule)

Proof. These follow directly from the definition and properties of derivatives of test functions. □

using FiniteDifferences, LinearAlgebra

# Numerical verification of distributional derivative properties
function verify_heaviside_derivative()
    # Approximate Heaviside function
    function heaviside_smooth(x, ε=0.01)
        return 0.5 * (1 + tanh(x/ε))
    end
    
    # Numerical derivative
    function numerical_derivative(f, x, h=1e-6)
        return (f(x+h) - f(x-h)) / (2h)
    end
    
    x_vals = -2:0.1:2
    ε_vals = [0.1, 0.05, 0.01, 0.005]
    
    println("Verification that H'(x) ≈ δ(x):")
    
    for ε in ε_vals
        # Compute numerical derivative of smoothed Heaviside
        derivatives = [numerical_derivative(t -> heaviside_smooth(t, ε), x) 
                      for x in x_vals]
        
        # The peak should approach δ(0) behavior
        peak_value = maximum(derivatives)
        peak_location = x_vals[argmax(derivatives)]
        width = length(findall(d -> d > peak_value/2, derivatives)) * 0.1
        
        println("ε = $ε: Peak = $peak_value at x = $peak_location, Width ≈ $width")
    end
end

verify_heaviside_derivative()

6.10 Fundamental Solutions and Green’s Functions

Distribution theory provides the natural framework for fundamental solutions of linear differential operators, which are distributional solutions to equations with Dirac delta forcing.

Definition 9.4 (Fundamental Solution). Let P(D) be a linear differential operator with constant coefficients. A distribution E ∈ D’(ℝⁿ) is called a fundamental solution of P(D) if P(D)E = δ in D’(ℝⁿ).

When a fundamental solution exists, it provides the building blocks for solving inhomogeneous equations P(D)u = f through convolution: u = E * f.

Example 9.3 (Heat Equation Fundamental Solution). For the heat operator ∂t - Δ in ℝⁿ⁺¹, the fundamental solution is: E(x,t) = { (4πt)^(-n/2) exp(-|x|²/(4t)) for t > 0 { 0 for t ≤ 0

This satisfies (∂t - Δ)E = δ(x)δ(t) in D’(ℝⁿ⁺¹).

Let us verify this fundamental solution property computationally:

using DifferentialEquations, Plots

# Heat equation fundamental solution in 1D
function heat_kernel(x, t, ε=1e-10)
    if t <= ε
        return 0.0
    else
        return (1/sqrt(4π*t)) * exp(-x^2/(4*t))
    end
end

# Verify that the heat kernel satisfies the heat equation away from t=0
function verify_heat_equation(x_range, t_vals)
    println("Verifying ∂E/∂t - ∂²E/∂x² ≈ 0 for t > 0:")
    
    for t in t_vals[t_vals .> 0]
        residuals = Float64[]
        
        for x in x_range
            # Numerical derivatives
            h = 1e-6
            dt = 1e-6
            
            E_t_plus = heat_kernel(x, t + dt)
            E_t_minus = heat_kernel(x, t - dt)
            ∂E_∂t = (E_t_plus - E_t_minus) / (2*dt)
            
            E_x_plus = heat_kernel(x + h, t)
            E_x = heat_kernel(x, t)
            E_x_minus = heat_kernel(x - h, t)
            ∂²E_∂x² = (E_x_plus - 2*E_x + E_x_minus) / h^2
            
            residual = ∂E_∂t - ∂²E_∂x²
            push!(residuals, residual)
        end
        
        max_residual = maximum(abs.(residuals))
        println("t = $t: max |∂E/∂t - ∂²E/∂x²| = $max_residual")
    end
end

x_range = -2:0.2:2
t_vals = [0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0]
verify_heat_equation(x_range, t_vals)

Theorem 9.3 (Malgrange-Ehrenpreis Theorem). Every linear partial differential operator P(D) with constant coefficients has a fundamental solution in D’(ℝⁿ).

The proof involves sophisticated techniques from complex analysis and algebraic geometry, constructing the fundamental solution via Fourier analysis and the theory of several complex variables.

6.10.1 Green’s Functions for Boundary Value Problems

For boundary value problems on bounded domains, Green’s functions provide the analog of fundamental solutions while satisfying homogeneous boundary conditions.

Definition 9.5 (Green’s Function). Let Ω ⊆ ℝⁿ be a bounded domain and P(D) a differential operator. The Green’s function G(x,y) satisfies: 1. P(D)G(·,y) = δ(· - y) in D’(Ω) 2. Appropriate homogeneous boundary conditions on ∂Ω

Example 9.4 (Poisson Equation Green’s Function). For -Δu = f in Ω with u = 0 on ∂Ω, the Green’s function satisfies: - -ΔₓG(x,y) = δ(x - y) in Ω - G(x,y) = 0 for x ∈ ∂Ω

The solution is then u(x) = ∫_Ω G(x,y)f(y)dy.

6.11 Fourier Transform of Distributions

The Fourier transform extends naturally to tempered distributions, providing powerful tools for analyzing distributions with polynomial growth.

6.11.1 Tempered Distributions

Definition 9.6 (Schwartz Space). The Schwartz space S(ℝⁿ) consists of all C^∞ functions φ such that for all multi-indices α, β: ||φ||{α,β} = sup{x∈ℝⁿ} |x^α ∂^β φ(x)| < ∞

Functions in S decay faster than any polynomial along with all their derivatives.

Definition 9.7 (Tempered Distribution). A tempered distribution is a continuous linear functional T: S(ℝⁿ) → ℝ. The space of tempered distributions is denoted S’(ℝⁿ).

Every distribution with compact support is tempered, as are distributions with polynomial growth like δ^(k) and polynomials.

Definition 9.8 (Fourier Transform of Tempered Distributions). For T ∈ S’(ℝⁿ), the Fourier transform T̂ is defined by ⟨T̂, φ⟩ = ⟨T, φ̂⟩ for all φ ∈ S(ℝⁿ).

Example 9.5 (Fourier Transform of Delta Function). ⟨δ̂, φ⟩ = ⟨δ, φ̂⟩ = φ̂(0) = ∫ φ(x)dx = ⟨1, φ⟩ Therefore δ̂ = 1, and by symmetry, 1̂ = (2π)ⁿδ.

This fundamental result shows that the Fourier transform of a point mass is a constant, while the Fourier transform of a constant is a point mass.

using FFTW, DSP

# Numerical verification of Fourier properties for distributions
function verify_delta_fourier_transform()
    N = 2^12
    L = 20.0  # Domain size
    dx = 2*L / N
    x = (-L:dx:L-dx)
    k = 2π * fftfreq(N, 1/dx)
    
    # Approximate delta function
    σ = 0.1
    δ_approx = (1/(σ*sqrt(2π))) * exp.(-x.^2 / (2*σ^2))
    
    # Fourier transform
    δ_hat = fftshift(fft(ifftshift(δ_approx))) * dx
    
    # Should approximate constant function
    constant_value = mean(real(δ_hat))
    deviation = std(real(δ_hat))
    
    println("Fourier transform of approximate δ:")
    println("Mean value: $constant_value")
    println("Standard deviation: $deviation")
    println("Theoretical value: $(dx)")  # Normalization factor
    
    # Plot results
    p1 = plot(x, δ_approx, title="Approximate δ function", 
              xlabel="x", ylabel="δ_σ(x)", lw=2)
    p2 = plot(k, real(δ_hat), title="Fourier transform", 
              xlabel="k", ylabel="Re[δ̂(k)]", lw=2)
    
    return plot(p1, p2, layout=(2,1))
end

verify_delta_fourier_transform()

Theorem 9.4 (Properties of Fourier Transform for Distributions). For S, T ∈ S’(ℝⁿ): 1. (∂^α T)^ = (2πi·)^α T̂ (differentiation becomes multiplication) 2. (x^α T)^ = (2πi)^{-|α|} ∂^α T̂ (multiplication becomes differentiation) 3. T̂̂(x) = (2π)ⁿ T(-x) (inversion formula) 4. (S * T)^ = Ŝ · T̂ when the convolution is well-defined

These properties make Fourier analysis a powerful tool for solving differential equations with constant coefficients.

6.12 Applications to Partial Differential Equations

Distribution theory provides rigorous foundations for many techniques used informally in applied mathematics and physics.

6.12.1 Singular Source Terms

Consider the Poisson equation with a point source: -Δu = δ(x - a) in ℝⁿ

The fundamental solution gives us immediately: u(x) = G(x - a)

where G is the fundamental solution of -Δ. In dimension n ≥ 3: G(x) = (1/((n-2)σₙ|x|^{n-2}))

where σₙ is the surface area of the unit sphere in ℝⁿ.

Example 9.6 (Electrostatics with Point Charges). The electric potential due to point charges qᵢ at positions xᵢ satisfies: -ε₀Δφ = Σᵢ qᵢδ(x - xᵢ)

The solution is φ(x) = (1/(4πε₀)) Σᵢ (qᵢ/|x - xᵢ|) in three dimensions.

using LinearAlgebra, Plots

# Solve Poisson equation with point sources using Green's function
function solve_poisson_point_sources(charges, positions, grid_points)
    n = length(grid_points)
    φ = zeros(n, n)
    x_vals = grid_points
    y_vals = grid_points
    
    for i in 1:n, j in 1:n
        x, y = x_vals[i], y_vals[j]
        point = [x, y]
        
        # Sum contributions from all point charges
        for (q, pos) in zip(charges, positions)
            r = norm(point - pos)
            if r > 1e-8  # Avoid singularity
                φ[i,j] += -q * log(r) / (2π)  # 2D Green's function
            end
        end
    end
    
    return φ
end

# Example: Two opposite charges
charges = [1.0, -1.0]
positions = [[-1.0, 0.0], [1.0, 0.0]]
grid_range = -3:0.1:3
φ = solve_poisson_point_sources(charges, positions, grid_range)

# Create contour plot
contour(grid_range, grid_range, φ', 
        title="Electric Potential from Point Charges",
        xlabel="x", ylabel="y", 
        levels=20, fill=true)

6.12.2 Wave Equations with Impulse Forcing

The wave equation with impulsive forcing demonstrates how distributions model instantaneous energy inputs:

∂²u/∂t² - c²Δu = δ(t)δ(x - a)

This represents an impulse delivered at time t = 0 and position x = a. The solution involves the retarded Green’s function, leading to wave propagation from the source point.

6.12.3 Heat Equations with Instantaneous Sources

For the heat equation with instantaneous heat input:

∂u/∂t - αΔu = δ(t)δ(x - a)

The solution u(x,t) = H(t)E(x-a,t) where H is the Heaviside function and E is the heat kernel, showing how an instantaneous point source diffuses over time.

6.13 Connections to Probability Theory

Distribution theory connects naturally to probability through the interpretation of measures as distributions.

Example 9.7 (Point Masses as Distributions). A probability measure μ with point masses pᵢ at locations xᵢ corresponds to the distribution T_μ = Σᵢ pᵢδ_{xᵢ}. For any test function φ: ⟨T_μ, φ⟩ = Σᵢ pᵢφ(xᵢ) = ∫ φ dμ

This connection allows distributional methods to be applied to problems involving discrete probability measures and empirical distributions.

6.14 Computational Implementation

using QuadGK, SpecialFunctions, LinearAlgebra

# General framework for working with distributions numerically
abstract type Distribution end

struct DiracDelta <: Distribution
    location::Float64
end

struct RegularDistribution <: Distribution
    f::Function
end

# Action of distribution on test function
function action(d::DiracDelta, φ::Function)
    return φ(d.location)
end

function action(d::RegularDistribution, φ::Function; domain=(-10, 10))
    integrand(x) = d.f(x) * φ(x)
    result, _ = quadgk(integrand, domain[1], domain[2])
    return result
end

# Example: Derivative of Heaviside
heaviside(x) = x >= 0 ? 1.0 : 0.0
H = RegularDistribution(heaviside)

# Test function
φ(x) = exp(-x^2) * sin(x)
φ_prime(x) = exp(-x^2) * (cos(x) - 2x*sin(x))

# Verify H' = δ
delta_action = action(DiracDelta(0.0), φ)
H_derivative_action = -action(H, φ_prime)

println("⟨δ, φ⟩ = $delta_action")
println("⟨H', φ⟩ = $H_derivative_action")
println("Difference: $(abs(delta_action - H_derivative_action))")

6.15 Exercises

  1. Computational Exercise: Implement a function to approximate the Dirac delta using a sequence of Gaussian functions δₑ(x) = (1/(ε√π))e^{-x²/ε²}. Verify numerically that ⟨δₑ, φ⟩ → φ(0) as ε → 0 for various test functions φ.

  2. Theoretical Exercise: Prove that if T ∈ D’(ℝ) has compact support, then T is a finite linear combination of derivatives of the Dirac delta: T = Σₖ₌₀ᴺ aₖδ^{(k)} for some coefficients aₖ and some N.

  3. Computational Exercise: Implement the fundamental solution for the 1D heat equation and use it to solve ∂u/∂t - ∂²u/∂x² = δ(x)δ(t) with zero initial conditions. Plot the solution at several time values.

  4. Mixed Exercise: Consider the distribution T defined by ⟨T, φ⟩ = φ’(0) - 2φ(1).

    • Show that T = δ’(x) - 2δ(x-1)
    • Compute the Fourier transform of T
    • Verify your result numerically

  5. Theoretical Exercise: Let f(x) = |x| on ℝ. Compute the distributional derivatives f’, f’‘, and f’’’ explicitly. Show that f’’’ = 2δ’(x).

  6. Computational Exercise: Use the Green’s function method to solve -Δu = sin(πx)sin(πy) on the unit square with u = 0 on the boundary. Compare your numerical solution with the exact solution.

  7. Advanced Exercise: Prove that every tempered distribution T ∈ S’(ℝⁿ) can be written as T = (1 - Δ)ᵏg for some k ∈ ℕ and some continuous function g with polynomial growth.

  8. Applications Exercise: Model the temperature distribution in a rod with instantaneous heat sources using distribution theory. Solve ∂u/∂t - ∂²u/∂x² = δ(t-1)δ(x-0.5) + δ(t-2)δ(x+0.5) with appropriate boundary conditions.

  9. Theoretical Exercise: Show that the convolution of two distributions S, T ∈ D’(ℝⁿ) is well-defined when at least one has compact support. Prove that S * T = T * S in this case.

  10. Computational Exercise: Implement a numerical method for computing the convolution of a distribution with compact support and a smooth function. Test your implementation on δ’ * e^{-x²}.

6.16 References

Hörmander, L. (1990). The Analysis of Linear Partial Differential Operators I: Distribution Theory and Fourier Analysis. Springer-Verlag.

Rudin, W. (1991). Functional Analysis. McGraw-Hill.

Schwartz, L. (1966). Théorie des Distributions. Hermann.

Strichartz, R. S. (1994). A Guide to Distribution Theory and Fourier Transforms. CRC Press.

Trèves, F. (2006). Topological Vector Spaces, Distributions and Kernels. Dover Publications.

Zemanian, A. H. (1987). Distribution Theory and Transform Analysis. Dover Publications.

7 Chapter 10: Approximation Theory and Universal Approximation

The fundamental problem of approximation theory is to find the best approximation to a given function from a specified class of approximating functions. This chapter develops the theoretical foundations of approximation in infinite-dimensional spaces and explores how these concepts apply to modern machine learning, particularly neural networks. We establish the mathematical framework for understanding why neural networks are such powerful function approximators and how they compare to classical approximation methods.

7.1 10.1 Best Approximation in Normed Spaces

7.1.1 10.1.1 Existence and Uniqueness of Best Approximations

Let X be a normed space with norm ‖·‖, and let M ⊆ X be a subset. For a given element x ∈ X, the distance from x to M is defined as

d(x, M) = inf{‖x - m‖ : m ∈ M}.

An element m₀ ∈ M is called a best approximation to x from M if ‖x - m₀‖ = d(x, M).

Definition 10.1 (Approximating Subset). A subset M of a normed space X is called approximating (or proximinal) if for every x ∈ X, there exists at least one best approximation to x from M.

The existence of best approximations requires additional structure on the approximating set.

Theorem 10.2 (Existence of Best Approximations). Let X be a normed space and M ⊆ X be a non-empty subset. If M is compact, then M is approximating.

Proof. Let x ∈ X be arbitrary. The function f(m) = ‖x - m‖ is continuous on M. Since M is compact, f attains its minimum on M. Let m₀ ∈ M be such that f(m₀) = min_{m∈M} f(m). Then ‖x - m₀‖ = d(x, M), so m₀ is a best approximation to x from M. □

In finite-dimensional spaces, closed and bounded sets are compact, making this theorem particularly useful. However, in infinite-dimensional spaces, we need stronger conditions.

Corollary 10.3. Let X be a finite-dimensional normed space and M ⊆ X be a finite-dimensional closed subset. Then M is approximating.

For uniqueness, we require additional geometric properties.

Definition 10.4 (Strictly Convex Space). A normed space X is strictly convex if for all x, y ∈ X with ‖x‖ = ‖y‖ = 1 and x ≠ y, we have ‖(x + y)/2‖ < 1.

Theorem 10.5 (Uniqueness of Best Approximations). Let X be a strictly convex normed space and M ⊆ X be a convex subset. Then for any x ∈ X, there exists at most one best approximation to x from M.

Proof. Suppose m₁, m₂ ∈ M are both best approximations to x, with m₁ ≠ m₂. Then ‖x - m₁‖ = ‖x - m₂‖ = d(x, M). Since M is convex, (m₁ + m₂)/2 ∈ M. By the strict convexity of X and the fact that ‖x - m₁‖ = ‖x - m₂‖, we have

‖x - (m₁ + m₂)/2‖ = ‖(x - m₁) + (x - m₂)‖/2 < max{‖x - m₁‖, ‖x - m₂‖} = d(x, M),

which contradicts the definition of distance to M. □

7.1.2 10.1.2 Characterization of Best Approximations

In Hilbert spaces, the characterization of best approximations becomes particularly elegant due to the projection theorem (Chapter 2).

Theorem 10.6 (Projection Theorem in Approximation). Let H be a Hilbert space and M ⊆ H be a non-empty closed convex subset. For any x ∈ H, there exists a unique best approximation P_M(x) ∈ M. Moreover, m₀ = P_M(x) if and only if

⟨x - m₀, m - m₀⟩ ≤ 0 for all m ∈ M.

If M is a closed subspace, this becomes ⟨x - m₀, m⟩ = 0 for all m ∈ M.

This projection theorem forms the foundation for many approximation algorithms and provides the theoretical basis for least-squares approximation.

7.1.3 10.1.3 Computational Example: Polynomial Approximation

Let’s implement best polynomial approximation in the L² sense using Julia.

using LinearAlgebra, Plots, QuadGK

"""
Compute L² best approximation by polynomials of degree ≤ n
using orthogonal polynomials (Legendre basis on [-1,1])
"""
function best_polynomial_approximation(f, n, interval=(-1, 1))
    a, b = interval
    
    # Transform to [-1, 1] for Legendre polynomials
    function transform_to_standard(x)
        return 2 * (x - a) / (b - a) - 1
    end
    
    function transform_from_standard(t)
        return a + (b - a) * (t + 1) / 2
    end
    
    # Legendre polynomials (normalized)
    function legendre(k, x)
        if k == 0
            return 1.0
        elseif k == 1
            return x
        else
            return ((2k - 1) * x * legendre(k-1, x) - (k - 1) * legendre(k-2, x)) / k
        end
    end
    
    # Compute coefficients using orthogonality
    coefficients = zeros(n + 1)
    
    for k in 0:n
        # Compute ⟨f, L_k⟩ where L_k is k-th Legendre polynomial
        integrand(t) = f(transform_from_standard(t)) * legendre(k, t)
        integral, _ = quadgk(integrand, -1, 1)
        
        # Normalize by ⟨L_k, L_k⟩ = 2/(2k+1)
        coefficients[k + 1] = integral * (2k + 1) / 2
    end
    
    # Return approximating polynomial
    function polynomial_approximation(x)
        t = transform_to_standard(x)
        result = 0.0
        for k in 0:n
            result += coefficients[k + 1] * legendre(k, t)
        end
        return result
    end
    
    return polynomial_approximation, coefficients
end

# Example: approximate exp(x) on [0, 1]
f(x) = exp(x)
degrees = [2, 4, 8]
x_plot = range(0, 1, length=100)

p = plot(x_plot, f.(x_plot), label="exp(x)", linewidth=2, color=:black)

for n in degrees
    poly_approx, _ = best_polynomial_approximation(f, n, (0, 1))
    plot!(p, x_plot, poly_approx.(x_plot), 
          label="degree $n", linewidth=1.5)
end

# Compute L² errors
for n in degrees
    poly_approx, _ = best_polynomial_approximation(f, n, (0, 1))
    error_func(x) = (f(x) - poly_approx(x))^2
    l2_error_squared, _ = quadgk(error_func, 0, 1)
    l2_error = sqrt(l2_error_squared)
    println("L² error for degree $n: $(l2_error)")
end

title!("Best L² Polynomial Approximations to exp(x)")
xlabel!("x")
ylabel!("y")

7.2 10.2 Kolmogorov n-Widths and Optimal Approximation

7.2.1 10.2.1 Definition and Basic Properties

Kolmogorov n-widths provide a measure of how well a compact set can be approximated by n-dimensional subspaces, giving fundamental limits on approximation rates.

Definition 10.7 (Kolmogorov n-Width). Let X be a normed space and K ⊆ X be a compact set. The Kolmogorov n-width of K in X is defined as

d_n(K, X) = inf{sup{d(x, L) : x ∈ K} : L ⊆ X, dim(L) ≤ n},

where d(x, L) = inf{‖x - l‖ : l ∈ L} is the distance from x to the subspace L.

The Kolmogorov n-width represents the best possible approximation error when approximating all elements of K using any n-dimensional subspace. It provides a benchmark against which specific approximation methods can be compared.

Theorem 10.8 (Properties of Kolmogorov n-Widths). Let K be a compact set in a normed space X. Then: 1. d₀(K, X) ≥ d₁(K, X) ≥ d₂(K, X) ≥ … ≥ 0 2. lim_{n→∞} d_n(K, X) = 0 3. If K has finite dimension m, then d_n(K, X) = 0 for all n ≥ m

7.2.2 10.2.2 Examples and Applications

Consider the unit ball in a Sobolev space W^{r,2}([0,1]) embedded in L²([0,1]). The behavior of Kolmogorov n-widths for such sets reveals fundamental limitations of approximation methods.

Theorem 10.9 (Kolmogorov n-Widths for Sobolev Balls). Let K be the unit ball in W^{r,2}([0,1]) viewed as a subset of L²([0,1]). Then

d_n(K, L²([0,1])) ≍ n^{-r},

where the notation f(n) ≍ g(n) means there exist constants c₁, c₂ > 0 such that c₁g(n) ≤ f(n) ≤ c₂g(n) for all n.

This theorem shows that functions with r derivatives have approximation rates proportional to n^{-r}, establishing the connection between smoothness and approximability.

using FFTW, LinearAlgebra

"""
Demonstrate Kolmogorov n-width decay for smooth functions
using Fourier series approximation on [0, 2π]
"""
function fourier_approximation_error(f, df_orders, n_terms, L=2π)
    # Compute Fourier coefficients for f and its derivatives
    N = 2048  # Number of sample points for FFT
    x = range(0, L, length=N+1)[1:end-1]  # Exclude endpoint for periodicity
    
    # Function values
    f_vals = f.(x)
    
    # FFT to get Fourier coefficients
    f_hat = fft(f_vals) / N
    
    # Frequencies
    freqs = fftfreq(N) * N * 2π / L
    
    errors = Float64[]
    
    for n in n_terms
        # Truncate to n terms (keeping DC and positive/negative frequencies balanced)
        f_hat_trunc = copy(f_hat)
        mask = abs.(freqs) .> n
        f_hat_trunc[mask] .= 0
        
        # Reconstruct approximation
        f_approx_vals = real.(ifft(f_hat_trunc * N))
        
        # Compute L² error
        error = sqrt(sum((f_vals - f_approx_vals).^2) * L / N)
        push!(errors, error)
    end
    
    return errors
end

# Example functions with different smoothness
f1(x) = abs(sin(x))^3  # Less smooth (finite differentiability)
f2(x) = sin(x) + 0.1 * sin(10*x)  # Smooth
f3(x) = exp(-x^2/2)  # Infinitely differentiable

n_values = [5, 10, 20, 40, 80]

errors1 = fourier_approximation_error(f1, [], n_values)
errors2 = fourier_approximation_error(f2, [], n_values)
errors3 = fourier_approximation_error(f3, [], n_values)

# Plot convergence rates
p = plot(n_values, errors1, label="abs(sin(x))³", 
         yscale=:log10, xscale=:log10, marker=:o)
plot!(p, n_values, errors2, label="sin(x) + 0.1sin(10x)", marker=:s)
plot!(p, n_values, errors3, label="exp(-x²/2)", marker=:^)

# Add reference lines showing different decay rates
plot!(p, n_values, 0.1 * n_values.^(-1), label="n⁻¹", linestyle=:dash)
plot!(p, n_values, 0.01 * n_values.^(-2), label="n⁻²", linestyle=:dash)

xlabel!("Number of Fourier modes")
ylabel!("L² approximation error")
title!("Approximation Error Decay Rates")
legend!()

7.3 10.3 Neural Network Universal Approximation

7.3.1 10.3.1 Classical Universal Approximation Theorem

The universal approximation theorem for neural networks, first proved by Cybenko (1989) and independently by Hornik (1991), establishes that feedforward neural networks with a single hidden layer can approximate any continuous function on a compact set to arbitrary accuracy.

Definition 10.10 (Feedforward Neural Network). A single-layer feedforward neural network with m hidden units is a function of the form

f(x) = Σ_{j=1}^m w_j σ(a_j^T x + b_j) + w_0,

where x ∈ ℝ^d is the input, σ: ℝ → ℝ is the activation function, w_j ∈ ℝ are output weights, a_j ∈ ℝ^d are input weights, b_j ∈ ℝ are biases, and w_0 ∈ ℝ is the output bias.

Theorem 10.11 (Cybenko’s Universal Approximation Theorem). Let σ be a continuous, bounded, and non-constant activation function. Let K ⊆ ℝ^d be compact and f: K → ℝ be continuous. Then for any ε > 0, there exists a single-layer neural network F of the form

F(x) = Σ_{j=1}^m w_j σ(a_j^T x + b_j) + w_0

such that sup_{x∈K} |f(x) - F(x)| < ε.

Proof Sketch. The proof uses the Riesz representation theorem and properties of signed measures. The key insight is that neural networks with sigmoidal activation functions can approximate indicator functions of half-spaces, and linear combinations of these can approximate any continuous function. We omit the technical details here but refer to Cybenko (1989) for the complete argument. □

7.3.2 10.3.2 Hornik’s Extension and Approximation of Derivatives

Hornik’s version extends beyond sigmoid functions and includes approximation of derivatives.

Theorem 10.12 (Hornik’s Universal Approximation Theorem). Let σ be any non-polynomial, bounded, and measurable activation function. Then neural networks with σ as activation function are dense in C(K) for any compact set K ⊆ ℝ^d.

Moreover, if σ is continuously differentiable, then neural networks can simultaneously approximate a function and its derivatives.

7.3.3 10.3.3 Barron’s Theorem and Approximation Rates

While the classical theorems establish existence of approximations, they don’t provide quantitative bounds. Barron (1993) gave the first approximation rate theorem for neural networks.

Definition 10.13 (Barron Class). A function f: ℝ^d → ℝ belongs to the Barron class if f has a representation

f(x) = ∫_{ℝ^d} e^{2πi ω^T x} μ(dω)

for some finite signed measure μ, and

C_f := ∫_{ℝ^d} ‖ω‖ |μ|(dω) < ∞,

where |μ| is the total variation of μ.

Theorem 10.14 (Barron’s Approximation Theorem). Let f belong to the Barron class with constant C_f, and let f_m be the best approximation to f by neural networks with m hidden units using sigmoidal activation functions. Then

‖f - f_m‖_{L²(B_R)} ≤ 2C_f R / √m,

where B_R is the ball of radius R in ℝ^d.

This theorem shows that for functions in the Barron class, neural networks achieve approximation rates that are independent of dimension d, circumventing the curse of dimensionality that affects traditional approximation methods.

7.3.4 10.3.4 Implementation: Universal Approximation in Practice

using Flux, Random, Plots, Statistics

# Generate synthetic data for approximation
function generate_target_function(x)
    return sin(2π * x[1]) * cos(π * x[2]) + 0.3 * sin(10π * x[1] * x[2])
end

# Create training data
function generate_data(n_samples, d, noise_level=0.01)
    Random.seed!(42)
    X = [2 * rand(d) .- 1 for _ in 1:n_samples]  # Uniform on [-1,1]^d
    y = [generate_target_function(x) + noise_level * randn() for x in X]
    return hcat(X...), y
end

# Neural network architectures of varying width
function create_network(input_dim, hidden_width)
    return Chain(
        Dense(input_dim, hidden_width, tanh),
        Dense(hidden_width, 1)
    )
end

# Training function
function train_network(model, X, y, epochs=1000)
    data = Flux.DataLoader((X, reshape(y, 1, :)), batchsize=32, shuffle=true)
    opt = Adam(0.001)
    
    losses = Float64[]
    
    for epoch in 1:epochs
        epoch_loss = 0.0
        for (x_batch, y_batch) in data
            loss, grads = Flux.withgradient(model) do m
                pred = m(x_batch)
                return Flux.mse(pred, y_batch)
            end
            Flux.update!(opt, model, grads[1])
            epoch_loss += loss
        end
        
        if epoch % 100 == 0
            avg_loss = epoch_loss / length(data)
            push!(losses, avg_loss)
            println("Epoch $epoch: Loss = $(avg_loss)")
        end
    end
    
    return losses
end

# Demonstrate universal approximation
d = 2  # Input dimension
n_train = 1000
X_train, y_train = generate_data(n_train, d)

# Test with different network widths
widths = [10, 25, 50, 100, 200]
test_errors = Float64[]

# Generate test data
n_test = 500
X_test, y_test = generate_data(n_test, d, 0.0)  # No noise for testing

for width in widths
    println("\nTraining network with width $width")
    model = create_network(d, width)
    
    # Train the network
    losses = train_network(model, X_train, y_train, 500)
    
    # Evaluate on test set
    y_pred = vec(model(X_test))
    test_error = sqrt(mean((y_test - y_pred).^2))
    push!(test_errors, test_error)
    
    println("Test RMSE: $test_error")
end

# Plot approximation quality vs network width
p = plot(widths, test_errors, marker=:o, linewidth=2,
         xlabel="Network Width (Hidden Units)", ylabel="Test RMSE",
         title="Universal Approximation: Error vs Network Width",
         yscale=:log10)

# Add theoretical decay rate (should be ∝ 1/√m for Barron functions)
theoretical_rate = test_errors[1] * sqrt(widths[1]) ./ sqrt.(widths)
plot!(p, widths, theoretical_rate, linestyle=:dash, 
      label="1/√m theoretical rate", color=:red)

display(p)

7.4 10.4 Comparison of Approximation Methods

7.4.1 10.4.1 Polynomial vs Neural Network Approximation

Traditional polynomial approximation suffers from the curse of dimensionality: to achieve error ε in dimension d, one typically needs O(ε^{-d}) parameters. Neural networks can achieve the same accuracy with O(ε^{-2}) parameters for functions in appropriate classes.

Theorem 10.15 (Curse of Dimensionality for Polynomials). Let f: [0,1]^d → ℝ be a function with bounded mixed derivatives up to order r in each variable. The error of best approximation by polynomials of degree n in each variable satisfies

inf_{p ∈ Π_n^d} ‖f - p‖_{L∞([0,1]d)} ≥ C n^{-r},

but the number of parameters required is O(n^d), leading to a parameter complexity of O(ε^{-d/r}) to achieve error ε.

In contrast, for functions in Barron’s class, neural networks require only O(ε^{-2}) parameters regardless of dimension.

7.4.2 10.4.2 Wavelets and Multiscale Approximation

Wavelet approximation provides another important comparison point, particularly effective for functions with localized features.

Definition 10.16 (Wavelet Approximation). Given a wavelet basis {ψ_{j,k}}, the wavelet approximation of f at level J is

f_J(x) = Σ_{k} c_{0,k} φ_{0,k}(x) + Σ_{j=0}^{J-1} Σ_k d_{j,k} ψ_{j,k}(x),

where φ is the scaling function and ψ is the mother wavelet.

For functions in Besov spaces B_p^{s,q}, wavelets achieve optimal approximation rates, but these rates still depend on dimension for fixed smoothness.

using Wavelets, LinearAlgebra

"""
Compare approximation methods: polynomials, wavelets, and neural networks
on functions with different characteristics
"""

# Test function with discontinuity (favorable for wavelets)
f_discontinuous(x) = sign(sin(4π * x[1])) * exp(-sum(x.^2))

# Smooth function (favorable for polynomials)
f_smooth(x) = exp(-2 * sum(x.^2)) * cos(3π * x[1]) * sin(2π * x[2])

# High-frequency function (challenging for all methods)
f_oscillatory(x) = sin(10π * sum(x)) / (1 + 5 * sum(x.^2))

function wavelet_approximation_1d(signal, wavelet_type=:db4, levels=3)
    """1D wavelet approximation for comparison"""
    wt = dwt(signal, wavelet(wavelet_type))
    
    # Keep only the largest coefficients
    n_keep = length(wt) ÷ 4  # Keep 25% of coefficients
    threshold = sort(abs.(wt), rev=true)[n_keep]
    
    wt_thresh = wt .* (abs.(wt) .≥ threshold)
    return idwt(wt_thresh, wavelet(wavelet_type))
end

# For demonstration, we'll compare 1D slices of our functions
x_1d = range(-1, 1, length=256)

# Test each function on a 1D slice
functions = [
    ("Discontinuous", x -> f_discontinuous([x, 0])),
    ("Smooth", x -> f_smooth([x, 0])),
    ("Oscillatory", x -> f_oscillatory([x, 0]))
]

for (name, func) in functions
    println("\nTesting function: $name")
    
    # Generate signal
    signal = func.(x_1d)
    
    # Polynomial approximation (using Chebyshev nodes)
    degrees = [8, 16, 32]
    poly_errors = Float64[]
    
    for deg in degrees
        # Simple polynomial fit using least squares
        A = hcat([x_1d.^i for i in 0:deg]...)
        coeffs = A \ signal
        poly_approx = A * coeffs
        error = sqrt(mean((signal - poly_approx).^2))
        push!(poly_errors, error)
    end
    
    # Wavelet approximation
    levels = [2, 3, 4]
    wavelet_errors = Float64[]
    
    for level in levels
        try
            wavelet_approx = wavelet_approximation_1d(signal, :db4, level)
            error = sqrt(mean((signal - wavelet_approx).^2))
            push!(wavelet_errors, error)
        catch
            push!(wavelet_errors, NaN)
        end
    end
    
    println("Polynomial errors (degrees $degrees): $poly_errors")
    println("Wavelet errors (levels $levels): $wavelet_errors")
end

7.5 10.5 Connection to Scientific Machine Learning

7.5.1 10.5.1 Neural Networks in Differential Equations

The universal approximation capabilities of neural networks make them particularly suitable for solving differential equations, leading to methods like Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) and Neural ODEs.

Definition 10.17 (Physics-Informed Neural Network). A PINN is a neural network f_θ(x, t) trained to satisfy a differential equation

∂u/∂t + N[u] = 0, x ∈ Ω, t ∈ [0, T],

with boundary conditions B[u] = 0 and initial conditions u(x, 0) = u₀(x), by minimizing the loss function

L(θ) = λ₁L_PDE(θ) + λ₂L_BC(θ) + λ₃L_IC(θ),

where each term penalizes violations of the PDE, boundary conditions, and initial conditions.

7.5.2 10.5.2 Approximation Theory Perspective on SciML

From an approximation theory perspective, neural networks in SciML benefit from:

  1. Universal approximation: Ability to represent complex solution manifolds
  2. Mesh-free discretization: No geometric constraints from fixed grids
  3. Automatic differentiation: Exact computation of derivatives needed for PDEs
  4. Optimization landscape: Gradient-based training finds good approximations
using DifferentialEquations, Flux, Zygote, Random

"""
Demonstrate neural network solution of ODE using universal approximation
Solve: du/dt = -2t*u, u(0) = 1 (exact solution: u(t) = exp(-t²))
"""

# Define the ODE
function ode_system(du, u, p, t)
    du[1] = -2*t*u[1]
end

# Exact solution for comparison
exact_solution(t) = exp(-t^2)

# Neural network ansatz
struct NeuralODE
    net::Chain
end

function (m::NeuralODE)(t)
    # Enforce initial condition: u(t) = 1 + t*NN(t)
    # This ensures u(0) = 1 regardless of NN weights
    return 1 + t * first(m.net([t]))
end

# Create neural network
hidden_dim = 20
neural_ode = NeuralODE(Chain(
    Dense(1, hidden_dim, tanh),
    Dense(hidden_dim, hidden_dim, tanh),
    Dense(hidden_dim, 1)
))

# Training data
t_train = range(0, 2, length=100)
t_data = reshape(collect(t_train), 1, :)

# Loss function: minimize ODE residual
function loss_function(model, t_points)
    total_loss = 0.0
    
    for t in t_points
        # Compute du/dt using automatic differentiation
        u_t = model(t)
        du_dt = gradient(t -> model(t), t)[1]
        
        # ODE residual: du/dt + 2t*u
        residual = du_dt + 2*t*u_t
        total_loss += residual^2
    end
    
    return total_loss / length(t_points)
end

# Training
optimizer = Adam(0.001)
losses = Float64[]

println("Training Neural ODE...")
for epoch in 1:2000
    loss, grads = Flux.withgradient(neural_ode) do model
        loss_function(model, t_train)
    end
    
    Flux.update!(optimizer, neural_ode, grads[1])
    
    if epoch % 200 == 0
        push!(losses, loss)
        println("Epoch $epoch: Loss = $loss")
    end
end

# Evaluate solution
t_test = range(0, 2, length=200)
u_neural = [neural_ode(t) for t in t_test]
u_exact = exact_solution.(t_test)

# Plot results
p1 = plot(t_test, u_exact, label="Exact solution", linewidth=2, color=:black)
plot!(p1, t_test, u_neural, label="Neural network", linewidth=2, linestyle=:dash, color=:red)
xlabel!("t")
ylabel!("u(t)")
title!("Neural ODE Solution")

# Plot error
p2 = plot(t_test, abs.(u_exact - u_neural), label="Absolute error", 
          linewidth=2, yscale=:log10, color=:blue)
xlabel!("t")
ylabel!("|u_exact - u_neural|")
title!("Approximation Error")

plot(p1, p2, layout=(2,1), size=(600, 400))

7.5.3 10.5.3 Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Spaces and Neural Networks

Recent work has connected neural networks to kernel methods through the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory, providing another approximation theory perspective.

Definition 10.18 (Neural Tangent Kernel). For a neural network f(x; θ) with parameters θ, the Neural Tangent Kernel is defined as

K_NTK(x, x’) = ∇_θ f(x; θ)^T ∇_θ f(x’; θ),

evaluated at initialization.

Under certain conditions (infinite width limits), neural network training becomes equivalent to kernel regression in the RKHS defined by the NTK, connecting neural network approximation theory to classical kernel methods.

7.6 10.6 Exercises

Exercise 10.1 (Computational). Implement best approximation in the uniform norm using the Remez exchange algorithm for polynomial approximation. Compare convergence rates with L² approximation for the function f(x) = |x| on [-1, 1].

Exercise 10.2 (Theoretical). Prove that in a Hilbert space, the best approximation from a closed convex set is unique.
